proposals.title: Comparative Bioburden on IV Line Connectors in ICU vs. General Ward Environments: A Prospective Observational Study
proposals.motivation: Infection prevention strategies, especially for device-related infections, are often developed and validated in high-resource ICU settings. It is assumed these protocols are universally superior and that ICU environments inherently have lower contamination risks. However, the high frequency of device manipulation and complex care in ICUs may paradoxically increase contamination opportunities for critical access points like IV line connectors, a known source of bloodstream infections. This assumption has not been rigorously tested against general ward practices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the frequency of manipulation and environmental factors in ICUs lead to higher bioburden on intravascular catheter connectors compared to those in general medical wards, challenging the blanket assumption of ICU protocol superiority for this specific task.
proposals.method: This will be a prospective, observational, multicenter study. We will sample intravascular catheter needleless connectors from a random selection of patients in ICUs and general medical wards at three timepoints over 24 hours. Sampling will use standardized pre-moistened swabs or direct agar imprint methods. Samples will be cultured quantitatively (CFU counts) and qualitatively (organism identification). Concurrently, trained observers will document connector manipulation events (e.g., medication administration, blood draws) and adherence to disinfection protocols using a standardized checklist. Analysis will compare mean CFU counts between settings using multivariate regression, controlling for manipulation frequency, patient diagnosis, and hospital site.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence on the real-world bioburden at a critical infection site across care settings, informing whether prevention strategies need to be context-specific. It will directly contribute to the evidence base for future guidelines on connector care, potentially advocating for enhanced or different protocols in high-manipulation environments like the ICU.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the common paradigm by testing if the high-intensity ICU environment might be a risk factor for connector contamination rather than a model of optimal practice, a non-obvious question given the extensive focus on ICU-specific prevention bundles.

proposals.title: A Mixed-Methods Analysis of Ward-Level Differences in Luer Lock Contamination: Exploring the Impact of Staffing, Workflow, and Environmental Factors on Bioburden in Acute Care vs. ICU Settings
proposals.motivation: Despite the critical role of infection prevention in all hospital settings, preliminary data from bioburden reduction studies indicate higher contamination levels on acute care wards compared to ICUs, a counterintuitive finding given the ICU's complexity. This disparity suggests that standard prevention strategies may not be uniformly effective across care environments. Understanding the underlying structural, procedural, and human factors driving this difference is essential for developing targeted, ward-specific interventions to reduce device-related infections and hospital-acquired pneumonia.
proposals.hypothesis: Higher luer lock contamination rates on acute care wards, compared to ICUs, are primarily driven by modifiable factors including higher nurse-to-patient ratios, less frequent use of closed-system access devices, and differences in the physical environment and workflow that increase touch contamination and reduce adherence to aseptic technique.
proposals.method: This prospective, observational cohort study will combine quantitative microbial sampling with qualitative workflow analysis. Over six months, we will collect standardized microbial cultures from luer locks of intravascular catheters and nearby high-touch surfaces (e.g., bedside tables, mobile phones) in matched acute care and ICU patient rooms. Concurrently, structured time-motion observations and anonymous surveys will assess nurse staffing ratios, procedural steps during line access, and availability of supplies. Data will be analyzed using multivariate logistic regression to identify independent predictors of contamination, controlling for patient acuity and diagnosis.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond documenting contamination rates to explain the 'why' behind ward-level differences, directly informing the 2022 strategies update for non-ventilator HAIs. It will generate actionable data for hospital administrators and infection preventionists to design context-aware protocols, potentially shifting resources or training to address specific vulnerabilities in acute care environments.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Cawcutt et al. has quantified contamination on devices like mobile phones, this study uniquely integrates environmental microbiology with human factors engineering to dissect the systemic causes of differential contamination between care settings, a directly comparative approach not previously undertaken.

proposals.title: A Multi-Center, International Assessment of Bioburden on Critical Care Equipment: Evaluating the Global Impact of a Standardized Decontamination Protocol
proposals.motivation: Healthcare-associated infections (HAIs), including ventilator-associated events and catheter-related bloodstream infections, remain a leading cause of morbidity and mortality, with environmental contamination being a key modifiable risk factor. Prior single-center studies, such as those on mobile phones and connectors, demonstrate significant bioburden but lack generalizability across different healthcare systems, resource settings, and infection prevention practices. A global assessment is critical to understand the universal versus context-specific challenges in equipment contamination.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a standardized, evidence-based decontamination protocol will significantly reduce microbial bioburden on high-touch critical care equipment across diverse international settings, but that the baseline contamination levels and protocol efficacy will vary significantly based on regional infection prevention infrastructure and compliance.
proposals.method: This will be a prospective, multi-center, international interventional study. Participating ICUs from hospitals in North America, Europe, and Asia will implement a standardized, multimodal decontamination protocol for high-touch equipment connectors (e.g., ventilator circuits, IV line connectors). The protocol will include defined cleaning agents, frequency, and audit tools. Pre- and post-intervention microbial cultures will be quantitatively analyzed using standardized laboratory methods (colony-forming unit counts and speciation). Data on local infection prevention policies, resource availability, and compliance audits will be collected. Statistical analysis will employ mixed-effects regression models to assess the intervention's effect while accounting for clustering by site and adjusting for regional covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, international data on the real-world bioburden of critical care equipment and the feasibility of a standardized decontamination strategy. It will identify global versus local barriers to effective environmental hygiene, directly informing the development of more resilient and adaptable infection prevention bundles for ventilator-associated events and other device-related HAIs.
proposals.novelty: While single-site connector studies exist, this is the first to systematically scale a bioburden reduction intervention across continents to disentangle the intervention's core efficacy from the moderating effects of healthcare system and cultural context, moving beyond proof-of-concept to practical implementation science.

proposals.title: Defining a Clinically Tolerable Threshold for Intravascular Catheter Connector Colonization: A Prospective Cohort Study to Inform Prevention Strategies
proposals.motivation: Intravascular catheter-related bloodstream infections (CRBSIs) remain a persistent source of morbidity and mortality. While connector colonization is a known precursor to CRBSI, current prevention strategies focus on elimination, leading to potentially excessive resource use. Defining an acceptable, non-harmful level of colonization would allow for more targeted and efficient infection prevention efforts in acute-care settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that there exists a quantifiable threshold of microbial bioburden on intravascular catheter connectors below which the risk of subsequent CRBSI is not significantly increased, and that this threshold can be defined using contemporary microbiological and clinical outcome data.
proposals.method: This will be a multi-center prospective cohort study of patients with central venous catheters in ICUs. Connectors will be systematically sampled at regular intervals using standardized swabbing and cultured with quantitative methods (CFU/mL). Patient outcomes will be tracked for CRBSI as defined by CDC/NHSN criteria. We will employ receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve analysis and multivariable logistic regression modeling to identify the colonization level (CFU threshold) that best discriminates between patients who do and do not develop CRBSI, adjusting for known confounders such as host factors and catheter dwell time.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first evidence-based, quantitative benchmark for 'acceptable' connector colonization, moving beyond a binary contaminated/sterile paradigm. It will directly inform future guidelines and stewardship of disinfection resources, helping clinicians prioritize interventions for high-risk colonization levels and reduce alarm fatigue associated with low-level bioburden.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the traditional problem from 'how to eliminate all bioburden' to 'how much bioburden is safe,' a novel risk-stratification approach for device-related infection prevention. It applies quantitative microbial risk assessment principles, common in environmental microbiology, to the specific clinical context of intravascular catheter care.

proposals.title: A Retrospective Cohort Study to Assess the Accuracy of NHSN CLABSI Definitions in Identifying Preventable Infections in the ICU
proposals.motivation: Central line-associated bloodstream infections (CLABSIs) remain a significant source of morbidity, mortality, and cost, with surveillance definitions driving public reporting and pay-for-performance metrics. However, concerns persist that the National Healthcare Safety Network (NHSN) definitions, while standardized, may misclassify infections, capturing non-preventable events and missing preventable ones. This misclassification can lead to inaccurate benchmarking, misdirected prevention resources, and potential unintended consequences for healthcare providers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of ICU patient events meeting the NHSN CLABSI definition represent non-preventable infections or non-infectious conditions, and conversely, that a subset of preventable, line-related infections are not captured by current surveillance criteria.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of adult ICU patients from 2023-2024. Using electronic health record data, we will identify all primary bloodstream infections (BSIs) meeting NHSN CLABSI criteria. A blinded, expert adjudication panel of infectious diseases physicians will review each case using a standardized protocol incorporating clinical, microbiological, and treatment data to classify events as 'Definitely Preventable,' 'Possibly Preventable,' or 'Not Preventable.' We will calculate the positive predictive value (PPV) of the NHSN definition for identifying 'Definitely/Possibly Preventable' infections. We will also perform a manual review of a random sample of ICU BSIs not meeting NHSN criteria to identify potential missed preventable events.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence quantifying the accuracy of current CLABSI surveillance definitions in identifying preventable harm. It will directly inform ongoing debates about the validity of CLABSI as a quality metric and the potential need for definition refinement. The findings will guide future efforts to develop more precise surveillance strategies that better align with true preventability.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has questioned CLABSI definitions, this study directly links adjudicated preventability to surveillance classification on a large scale, moving beyond anecdote to measurable misclassification rates. It applies a rigorous, clinical adjudication framework—common in diagnostic studies—to the evaluation of an administrative surveillance definition.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Patient-Family Empowerment Bundle on Central Line-Associated Bloodstream Infection (CLABSI) Rates in the ICU
proposals.motivation: Central line-associated bloodstream infections (CLABSIs) remain a persistent source of morbidity, mortality, and cost, despite established prevention bundles. Pre-2026 evidence shows bundle compliance gaps persist, and patient engagement is an underutilized strategy. This study leverages the patient and family as active auditors of care to address these compliance gaps.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a structured patient-family empowerment intervention, including education and a real-time questioning tool for central line care, will increase adherence to CLABSI prevention protocols and lead to a significant reduction in CLABSI rates compared to standard care alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-arm, cluster-randomized controlled trial in adult ICUs. The intervention arm will receive a multi-component bundle: 1) an educational video for patients/families on CLABSI prevention, 2) a bedside poster with key care questions (e.g., "Has everyone washed their hands?"), and 3) a structured script for nurses to invite questions. The control arm will receive standard care. The primary outcome is CLABSI rate per 1,000 central line days. Secondary outcomes include direct observation of bundle compliance (hand hygiene, dressing integrity) and surveys of patient/family and staff perceptions. Analysis will use intention-to-treat and mixed-effects models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide high-level evidence on the efficacy of scaling patient-family engagement as a direct infection prevention strategy. It will yield a practical, implementable toolkit for hospitals and contribute to the understanding of human factors and safety culture in sustaining bundle compliance.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on clinician-facing strategies, this trial directly tests a standardized, scalable intervention that transforms patients and families from passive recipients into active, empowered participants in auditing central line care, a novel application of engagement principles to a hard clinical outcome.

proposals.title: Decoupling Histopathology and Molecular Detection: A Prospective Cohort Study on the Clinical Significance of PCR-Positive, PAS-Negative Tissue in Suspected Invasive Fungal Infections
proposals.motivation: Diagnosis of invasive fungal infections (IFIs) relies heavily on histopathology (e.g., Periodic Acid-Schiff stain) and culture, which have limited sensitivity. Molecular methods like PCR offer higher sensitivity but their clinical relevance when histopathology is negative remains unclear. This diagnostic discordance creates uncertainty in patient management, potentially delaying appropriate antifungal therapy or leading to unnecessary treatment, impacting outcomes in critically ill and immunocompromised populations.
proposals.hypothesis: PCR-positive, PAS-negative tissue samples represent true, sub-microscopic fungal burden with distinct clinical correlates, including specific host immune phenotypes and subsequent progression to overt IFI, rather than solely contamination or non-viable DNA.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective observational cohort study of patients undergoing tissue biopsy for suspected IFI. All samples will undergo standard histopathology/culture and broad-range fungal PCR/sequencing. For discordant (PCR+/PAS-) cases, we will perform host transcriptional profiling (RNA-seq) to characterize local immune response and correlate findings with detailed clinical data, including serial biomarkers (e.g., beta-D-glucan), antifungal exposure, and 30-day outcomes. Statistical analysis will use multivariate regression to identify factors predictive of progression to proven/probable IFI (per 2008 EORTC/MSG criteria) in discordant cases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide evidence-based guidance on interpreting discordant fungal diagnostic results, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship and personalized treatment strategies. It will advance the understanding of early or contained fungal invasion and may validate PCR as a critical tool for pre-emptive therapy, particularly in high-risk patients with negative histopathology.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies note PCR-histopathology discordance, this proposal systematically investigates its biological and clinical meaning by integrating host-response profiling with longitudinal outcomes, moving beyond mere detection to mechanistic and prognostic insight.

proposals.title: Optimizing Syndromic PCR Panels for Early Sepsis Diagnosis in Ventilated Patients: A Prospective Cohort Study to Define Site-Specific Pathogen Yield
proposals.motivation: In critically ill, ventilated patients, early and accurate diagnosis of infection is crucial for guiding antimicrobial therapy and improving outcomes. Current practices for obtaining samples for PCR testing (e.g., blood, respiratory, urine) are often empiric, leading to potential delays, unnecessary broad-spectrum antibiotic use, and missed diagnoses if the wrong site is sampled. Defining an evidence-based, phenotype-driven approach to PCR panel selection could streamline diagnosis and align with antimicrobial stewardship goals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific clinical phenotypes (e.g., new hypoxemia, hemodynamic instability, leukocytosis with fever) will be associated with distinct, optimal combinations of tissue and fluid sample sites (e.g., bronchoalveolar lavage, blood, sterile site fluid) for multiplex PCR testing, maximizing pathogen detection yield compared to standard, non-phenotype-driven sampling.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective observational cohort study of ventilated patients in the ICU with suspected infection. For each patient, we will record a standardized clinical phenotype at the time of suspicion. We will then obtain a pre-specified bundle of samples (blood, endotracheal aspirate, urine) for both standard culture and broad multiplex PCR panels (e.g., BioFire® FilmArray® Pneumonia plus, Blood Culture Identification panels). Using multivariate logistic regression and classification tree analysis, we will model the relationship between clinical phenotypes and PCR-positivity at each site to identify the most informative sampling strategy for each phenotype.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a data-driven protocol for clinicians to select the most high-yield PCR panels based on a patient's presenting signs, potentially reducing time-to-diagnosis and diagnostic ambiguity. It will directly inform antimicrobial stewardship by promoting targeted testing and therapy, building upon Dr. Cawcutt's prior work in infection prevention and ventilator-associated event strategies.
proposals.novelty: While PCR panels are widely available, the strategic selection of which panel to use based on syndromic presentation is not standardized. This study moves beyond validating individual tests to creating an integrative clinical decision framework that links specific host responses to optimal diagnostic pathways.

proposals.title: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of the Association Between Healthcare Workforce Compensation Structures and Adherence to Infection Prevention Strategies
proposals.motivation: Healthcare-associated infections (HAIs), including ventilator-associated events, remain a persistent source of patient morbidity and mortality. Pre-2026 literature robustly links staffing levels and burnout to HAI rates, but the specific impact of compensation models (e.g., salary, incentives, differentials) on the consistent application of evidence-based prevention strategies is poorly characterized. Understanding this relationship is critical for designing systemic interventions that support the frontline workforce in delivering reliable, high-quality care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that compensation structures positively associated with professional satisfaction and retention (e.g., competitive base pay, clinical ladder incentives) will correlate with higher observed adherence to core infection prevention bundles across nursing, pharmacy, and respiratory therapy roles.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic review and meta-analysis following PRISMA guidelines. We will search PubMed, CINAHL, EconLit, and PsycINFO (from inception to December 2025) for studies reporting both compensation variables and measurable outcomes related to adherence to infection prevention protocols (e.g., hand hygiene, central line bundle compliance, ventilator bundle compliance). Two independent reviewers will screen, extract data, and assess quality using the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale. Quantitative synthesis will use random-effects meta-analysis models in R (metafor package) to pool correlation coefficients or odds ratios, with subgroup analyses by profession and infection type.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first synthesized evidence linking economic workforce factors directly to procedural adherence in infection prevention. It will move the discourse beyond staffing numbers to the quality of staff support, offering hospital administrators and policymakers a novel evidence-based lever (compensation design) to potentially improve patient safety outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Cawcutt's prior work has focused on clinical strategies and device-related interventions, this proposal novelly integrates health services research and health economics into the infection prevention paradigm, specifically examining compensation—a foundational but understudied systemic factor—across the multi-professional team.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Home-Based Immunophenotyping to Predict Infection-Associated Chronic Pain in Patients on Long-Term Intravenous Therapy
proposals.motivation: Patients on long-term home IV therapy are at high risk for catheter-related bloodstream infections (CRBSI), which can trigger complex immunological responses. Pre-2026 evidence links post-infectious immune dysregulation, including bystander activation and molecular mimicry, to the development of chronic neuropathic and musculoskeletal pain syndromes. Current home care focuses on procedural prevention of infections, leaving a critical gap in predicting and mitigating their long-term painful sequelae in this vulnerable, home-bound population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that longitudinal, home-collected immunophenotypic data will reveal distinct biomarker signatures predictive of progression from an acute CRBSI to chronic, infection-triggered pain. Further, we hypothesize that integrating these signatures into a clinical decision algorithm will enable pre-emptive intervention to reduce chronic pain incidence.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of 150 patients on long-term home IV therapy. Using a modified home care checklist, nurses will perform serial point-of-care capillary blood sampling during routine visits. Samples will be analyzed via pre-2026 multiplex cytokine/chemokine panels (e.g., IL-6, IL-17, IFN-γ, CXCL10) and flow cytometry for T-cell subsets (e.g., Th17, CD8+ effector memory). Data will be integrated with infection events and validated pain scores (BPI). A machine learning algorithm (e.g., a random forest classifier) will be trained to identify predictive immune trajectories for chronic pain development.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first longitudinal map of post-infectious immune trajectories in the home IV therapy population, directly linking CRBSI immunology to pain outcomes. It will produce a novel, actionable clinical decision support tool that shifts home care from reactive infection management to predictive immunologic risk stratification. The findings will inform targeted immunomodulatory strategies to prevent chronic pain.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely fuses Dr. Cawcutt's expertise in infection prevention and vascular care with immunophenotyping in the novel setting of the home, moving beyond hospital-acquired infection rates to address long-term patient-centered outcomes like chronic pain. The application of predictive analytics to home-collected immune data for pain risk is a previously unexplored paradigm.

proposals.title: Optimizing Diagnostic Pathways for Infectious Etiologies in Chronic Pain: A Discrete-Event Simulation Study to Guide Clinical Research Protocols
proposals.motivation: Chronic pain syndromes without a clear etiology are a major clinical burden, and occult infections are a recognized but often underexplored contributor. Current diagnostic approaches are often sequential, costly, and slow, leading to delayed treatment and prolonged patient suffering. There is a critical need for evidence-based, resource-efficient diagnostic strategies to systematically investigate infectious causes in this population.
proposals.hypothesis: A discrete-event simulation model, parameterized with real-world clinical and laboratory data, can identify an optimized diagnostic cascade that maximizes the efficient identification of infectious etiologies in chronic pain patients while minimizing resource utilization and diagnostic delay.
proposals.method: We will develop a discrete-event simulation model using software like SimPy or AnyLogic. The model will simulate patient flow through diagnostic pathways, incorporating nodes for tests (e.g., syndromic multiplex PCR panels, serologic assays, cultures), specialist consultations, and result waiting times. Input parameters (test characteristics, costs, turnaround times) will be derived from institutional data and literature. We will use a design-of-experiments approach to evaluate and compare the performance of different diagnostic strategies (e.g., broad PCR first vs. tiered testing) against outcome measures including diagnostic yield, time-to-diagnosis, and total cost.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, flexible simulation tool to rationally design clinical research protocols for investigating infections in chronic pain. It will provide a methodology to pre-emptively balance diagnostic thoroughness with practical constraints of cost and time, directly informing more efficient study designs. The findings will offer a framework for healthcare systems to implement standardized, cost-effective diagnostic algorithms.
proposals.novelty: While simulation is used for hospital operations and infection prevention, applying discrete-event modeling specifically to optimize the diagnostic workup for infectious causes of chronic pain is a novel integration of operational research and clinical infectious disease. It moves beyond descriptive guidelines to a dynamic, quantitative tool for pathway design.

proposals.title: Disentangling Viral from Contextual Drivers of Post-Pandemic Chronic Pain: A Stressor-Matched Prospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Chronic pain is a widely reported sequela of SARS-CoV-2 infection, but its etiology remains confounded. The pandemic itself created profound, population-wide psychosocial and economic stressors, which are independent, well-established risk factors for chronic pain development. It is therefore critical to determine whether post-COVID pain is a direct pathophysiological consequence of the virus or a manifestation of the pandemic's contextual trauma, as this distinction directly informs prevention and care strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: The incidence and severity of new-onset chronic pain will be significantly higher in individuals with confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection compared to a cohort matched for exposure to severe non-infection pandemic stressors, after controlling for baseline demographics and pre-pandemic pain history.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-arm prospective cohort study. Group 1 (n=250) will comprise adults with confirmed COVID-19 (PCR+). Group 2 (n=250) will be stressor-matched controls without prior infection but with verified exposure to significant pandemic-era disruptions (e.g., job loss, bereavement, severe social isolation). Group 3 (n=250) will be a pre-pandemic historical control cohort from existing biobank data. All participants undergo baseline deep phenotyping, including the Perceived Stress Scale, Pandemic Stressor Index, and quantitative sensory testing (QST). They will be followed longitudinally for 12 months with bimonthly assessments using the Brief Pain Inventory and PROMIS Pain Interference scales. Core analysis will use multivariable Cox proportional hazards and mixed-effects models to compare pain incidence and trajectories between Groups 1 and 2.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct evidence quantifying the specific contribution of SARS-CoV-2 infection to chronic pain risk, isolated from the background 'noise' of pandemic-related stress. It will inform more targeted clinical guidelines for post-COVID syndrome management and refine public health resource allocation for long-term pandemic sequelae. The stressor-matching methodology will also serve as a novel framework for studying other post-infectious syndromes in complex societal contexts.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies describe pain after COVID-19, none have employed a rigorously matched control group exposed to identical contextual stressors but not the virus itself, which is essential for causal attribution. This design directly addresses the critical confounder of pandemic-era trauma that has been largely ignored in the existing literature.

proposals.title: Crowdsourcing Clinical Practice: A Social Media-Based Framework for Real-Time Refinement of Ventilator-Associated Event Prevention Strategies
proposals.motivation: Infection prevention guidelines, such as those for ventilator-associated events (VAEs), are traditionally static, updated infrequently via expert consensus. This creates a lag in incorporating novel frontline clinical observations and local adaptations, especially for complex care bundles. Social media platforms like X (formerly Twitter) and specialized forums host vibrant, real-time discussions among healthcare professionals about practical challenges and informal solutions, representing an untapped reservoir of experiential knowledge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic analysis of healthcare professional discussions on social media can identify emerging, context-specific adaptations of VAE prevention strategies, and that these insights can be structured to create a dynamic, supplementary knowledge base that evolves more rapidly than formal guidelines.
proposals.method: We will collect publicly available posts from X and clinician forums using targeted keywords related to VAE prevention (e.g., #ventilator, oral care, sedation vacation). Using a pre-2026 natural language processing pipeline (e.g., BERT-based models for topic modeling and sentiment analysis), we will categorize discussions into themes (e.g., adherence barriers, device modifications). A validated infection prevention ontology will be used to map informal descriptions to standardized concepts. A web-based dashboard will visualize the frequency and co-occurrence of these adapted practices over time, allowing for real-time monitoring.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept for a novel surveillance system that captures the dynamic evolution of bedside infection prevention practices. It will generate a new methodology for translating unstructured frontline discourse into structured, actionable data that can inform guideline updates and implementation science.
proposals.novelty: While social media has been studied for public health surveillance of diseases, its application for crowdsourcing the *evolution of best practices* from healthcare workers themselves is novel. This moves beyond monitoring compliance to capturing innovation and adaptation in near real-time.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Dynamic, Multimodal Machine Learning Model for Individualized Midline Catheter Dwell Time Guidance
proposals.motivation: Midline catheters are essential for vascular access but carry risks of failure, infection, and thrombosis, leading to patient harm and increased healthcare costs. Current practice relies on fixed dwell time recommendations, which are reactive and fail to account for individual patient physiology and evolving catheter conditions. A proactive, precision-based strategy is needed to optimize dwell time and prevent complications, aligning with Dr. Cawcutt's focus on preventive strategies for device-associated infections.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model integrating serial vascular ultrasound, biomarkers of endothelial irritation (e.g., D-dimer, IL-6), infusion characteristics, and patient comorbidity data can accurately predict the real-time risk of midline catheter failure, enabling individualized removal decisions that reduce complication rates compared to standard time-based protocols.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of patients with midline catheters, collecting multimodal data at insertion and serially until removal. The primary outcome is catheter failure (composite of symptomatic thrombosis, infection, occlusion, or infiltration). We will train a time-varying survival model, such as a dynamic DeepHit or a recurrent neural network (RNN) with attention mechanisms, to process the longitudinal data and output a continuously updated risk score. Model performance will be validated against a held-out test set using time-dependent AUC and calibration metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, data-driven tool to move midline catheter management from a static, time-based paradigm to dynamic, physiology-guided precision care. It will directly inform clinical decision-making for catheter maintenance, with the potential to reduce complication rates, improve patient outcomes, and decrease unnecessary catheter removals. The model framework could be adapted for other intravascular devices.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in its integration of serial, real-time physiological data (especially vascular ultrasound and biomarkers) into a dynamic risk-prediction model for midline catheters, moving beyond static risk factors or simple dwell time rules used in prior studies.

proposals.title: A Multicenter, Prospective Cohort Study to Define the Optimal Dwell Time and Risk Factors for Midline Catheter Failure and Complications
proposals.motivation: Midline catheters are increasingly used for intermediate-term vascular access, but evidence-based guidelines for their maximum safe dwell time are lacking. Pre-2026 practice is guided by manufacturer recommendations (often 14-29 days) and expert opinion, not high-quality clinical data, leading to potential variability in care, premature removal, or delayed recognition of complications like catheter-associated bloodstream infection (CLABSI) or thrombosis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that there is a time-dependent increase in the risk of midline catheter failure (defined as removal due to complication or dysfunction) after a specific dwell time threshold, and that this risk is modified by patient-specific and insertion-related factors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational cohort study across 5-10 acute-care hospitals. All adult patients receiving a midline catheter per standard care will be enrolled. Data on patient demographics, insertion technique, dwell time, and daily catheter assessment (for pain, signs of infection, patency) will be collected. The primary outcome is catheter failure. We will use survival analysis (Kaplan-Meier curves and Cox proportional hazards regression) to model the time-to-failure and identify the dwell time point where the hazard function increases significantly, controlling for confounders like site, indication, and nurse inserter training.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, prospective data to define an evidence-based maximum recommended dwell time for midline catheters, directly informing future clinical guidelines. It will also identify modifiable risk factors for complications, enabling targeted prevention strategies to improve device safety and patient outcomes, aligning with Dr. Cawcutt's focus on infection prevention and vascular access care.
proposals.novelty: While extensive literature exists on CLABSI prevention for central lines and strategies for peripheral IVs, the optimal dwell time for midlines—a distinct device category—remains an unaddressed, critical knowledge gap. This study moves beyond descriptive audits to apply time-to-event analysis specifically to define a safety threshold for this device.

proposals.title: Integrating Pre-Existing Chronic Pain and Immune Phenotype into a Patient-Specific Risk Model for Central Line-Associated Bloodstream Infections
proposals.motivation: Despite comprehensive bundles, device-related infections like CLABSI remain a major cause of morbidity and mortality in critical care. Current risk models rely heavily on demographic and clinical factors, failing to account for patient-specific biological susceptibility. Chronic pain is highly prevalent in hospitalized patients and is known, based on pre-2026 immunology, to induce a pro-inflammatory state and dysregulate immune cell function, creating a plausible biological pathway for increased infection risk that has not been incorporated into predictive models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pre-existing chronic pain, through quantifiable alterations in systemic immune function (e.g., elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines, impaired neutrophil extracellular trap formation), is a causal, modifiable risk factor that significantly increases the probability of developing a central line-associated bloodstream infection (CLABSI).
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational cohort study of adults requiring central venous catheterization in the ICU. Pre-insertion, we will quantify chronic pain burden using validated scales (e.g., Brief Pain Inventory) and measure immune phenotypes via serum cytokine panels (IL-6, TNF-α, IL-10) and ex vivo leukocyte functional assays. We will develop a multivariable time-to-event (Cox proportional hazards) model, integrating pain scores and immune biomarkers with traditional clinical risk factors (e.g., APACHE II, catheter dwell time). Model performance will be assessed using C-statistics and calibration plots, with causal mediation analysis used to test the pathway from pain to immune phenotype to infection.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first predictive model for CLABSI that incorporates a patient-specific, biologically plausible immunomodulatory mechanism. It will provide evidence for chronic pain as a novel, causal risk factor, moving beyond correlation. The resulting model will enable clinicians to stratify patients by mechanistic risk, guiding targeted pre-emptive strategies such as optimized analgesia, immunomodulatory consideration, or enhanced device care for high-risk phenotypes.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely posits pre-existing pain not merely as a comorbidity but as a direct causal immunomodulator driving device infection risk, and it integrates real-time immune phenotyping into a clinical prediction model to create a mechanism-aware tool for personalized prevention.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Machine Learning Model for Patient-Specific Risk Prediction of Intravascular Catheter-Related Complications
proposals.motivation: Intravascular catheter-related bloodstream infections (CRBSIs) remain a persistent source of morbidity, mortality, and cost in acute-care hospitals, despite established prevention bundles. Current strategies are broadly applied, but individual patient risk varies significantly based on clinical, demographic, and device-specific factors. A patient-specific risk prediction tool could enable more precise, preemptive care by guiding optimal vascular access device selection and targeted prevention strategies for high-risk patients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model trained on electronic health record (EHR) data can accurately predict an individual patient's risk of developing a CRBSI or other major catheter-related complication (e.g., thrombosis, mechanical failure) within 72 hours of vascular access device placement.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using EHR data from adult patients admitted to ICUs and acute-care wards from 2018-2024. Features will include demographics, comorbidities (Charlson index), laboratory values (e.g., leukocyte count, creatinine), indication for access, device type/location, concurrent treatments (e.g., vasopressors, sedation), and prior device history. The primary outcome is a composite of CRBSI (per NHSN criteria) or device failure. We will train and compare several algorithms, including logistic regression with regularization (LASSO), random forest, and gradient boosting machines (XGBoost), using temporal validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, clinically actionable risk score that can be integrated into EHR systems to provide real-time decision support at the point of vascular access ordering. It moves infection prevention from a one-size-fits-all bundle to a personalized, risk-stratified approach, potentially reducing complications and unnecessary device use. The model will also identify novel, modifiable risk factors beyond those in current guidelines.
proposals.novelty: While prior models predict CRBSI risk, they are often limited to specific device types (e.g., central lines) or patient populations (e.g., ICU). This model is novel in its comprehensive scope across all vascular access devices (peripheral, midline, central) and its use of contemporary machine learning techniques on rich, real-time EHR data to generate dynamic, patient-specific predictions at the time of clinical decision-making.

proposals.title: A Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis Framework for Immunological Stratification and Resource-Aware Management of Post-Infection Pain Syndromes
proposals.motivation: Post-infection pain syndromes (PIPS), including fibromyalgia and neuropathic pain, represent a significant and costly burden, often with heterogeneous immune pathophysiology. Current management is largely empirical, lacking tools to integrate complex immunological data with practical clinical and economic constraints for personalized care. This gap leads to prolonged patient suffering and inefficient resource use, a critical issue in acute-care and post-discharge settings.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) to integrate clinical severity, prognostic biomarker value, and treatment cost-effectiveness will generate a valid stratification tool that optimizes personalized management strategies for patients with PIPS.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of patients with PIPS, collecting clinical data, pain metrics, and serum/CSF for cytokine/autoantibody profiling. An expert panel will define MCDA criteria (e.g., biomarker specificity, treatment cost, accessibility) and weights via the Analytic Hierarchy Process. The TOPSIS algorithm will then rank patient-specific immune profiles (e.g., high bystander activation vs. molecular mimicry signatures) against ideal therapeutic strategies, generating a decision-support output.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, quantitative framework to move from descriptive immunology to actionable clinical decisions in PIPS. It will directly contribute a pragmatic tool for stratifying patients into management pathways that balance biological drivers with healthcare resource utilization, aligning with value-based care models.
proposals.novelty: While MCDA is used in health technology assessment, its application to integrate mechanistic immunology with real-world clinical and economic decision-making for chronic post-infection sequelae is novel, creating a bridge between pathophysiology and implementation science.

proposals.title: A Pilot Study of a Remote Monitoring and Decision-Support Algorithm for Home-Based Antimicrobial Infusion in Chronic Pain Patients with a History of Catheter-Related Infections
proposals.motivation: Patients with chronic pain requiring long-term vascular access for therapies face significant risks of catheter-related bloodstream infections (CRBSI), often leading to hospitalization. Current home infusion practices rely on static protocols and reactive care, lacking dynamic, personalized adaptation to early signs of infection or treatment response. This gap is critical, as it impacts patient safety, drives antimicrobial overuse, and increases healthcare utilization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a closed-loop decision-support algorithm, integrating real-time physiological data from connected infusion devices with established clinical biomarkers, will enable safer, more precise home-based antimicrobial administration for chronic pain patients with prior infections, reducing adverse events and unplanned hospital visits compared to standard care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pilot randomized controlled trial. The intervention arm will use a hybrid algorithm deployed on a secure cloud platform. This algorithm will fuse continuous data (e.g., temperature, infusion pressure from smart pumps) with periodic patient-reported outcomes (pain scores) and scheduled biomarker inputs (e.g., procalcitonin, C-reactive protein via point-of-care testing). A rules-based engine, informed by 2022 ventilator-associated event prevention criteria and antimicrobial stewardship principles, will generate alerts and recommend regimen adjustments to clinicians. The control arm will receive standard home infusion nursing support.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, integrated monitoring framework for home infusion, directly extending hospital-based infection prevention strategies to the community. It will provide preliminary evidence on the feasibility and safety of algorithm-guided, precision antimicrobial use in a high-risk chronic pain population, a previously unstudied application.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in its fusion of real-time device data with biomarker-driven stewardship for outpatient chronic pain management, moving beyond static protocols to create an adaptive, remote-care model specifically tailored for infection-prone patients, which has not been previously explored.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Clinical Decision Algorithm to Minimize Invasive Diagnostic Procedures in the Search for Occult Infection in Patients with Chronic Pain
proposals.motivation: Chronic pain patients with suspected occult infection (e.g., prosthetic joint infection, spinal osteomyelitis) face a diagnostic dilemma: exhaustive invasive sampling (biopsies, aspirations) carries procedural risks and patient burden, while delayed diagnosis leads to prolonged morbidity. Current diagnostic strategies are often sequential and not formally optimized to minimize invasive procedures. This problem mirrors the high-stakes risk-benefit calculus seen in preventing device-related infections (e.g., ventilator-associated pneumonia, catheter-related bloodstream infections), where procedural burden must be balanced against infection risk.
proposals.hypothesis: A Bayesian clinical decision algorithm that integrates pre-test patient data to sequentially update the probability of infection at candidate anatomical sites will identify the optimal, minimal-number sequence of invasive diagnostic procedures needed to achieve a target diagnostic confidence, thereby reducing overall procedural burden.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian network model integrating pre-2026 validated variables: patient history (fever, immunosuppression), imaging findings (MRI/CT localization), and non-invasive biomarkers (CRP, ESR, leukocyte count). The algorithm will treat each potential sampling site as a node with a prior probability of yielding a pathogen, updated after each simulated or real procedural result. Using a decision tree optimized with a cost function that penalizes both procedural risk (complication rates) and diagnostic delay, the model will output a patient-specific ranked list of sites for sampling. Validation will occur via retrospective simulation using a cohort of chronic pain patients who underwent exhaustive diagnostic workup.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, ethical framework for personalizing the diagnostic search for occult infection, directly reducing unnecessary invasive device insertions (biopsy needles) and associated complications. It translates principles of infection prevention and diagnostic stewardship from critical care (e.g., ventilator strategies) into the chronic pain setting, offering a structured protocol to improve patient safety and resource utilization.
proposals.novelty: While decision aids exist, applying a formal Bayesian optimization framework to explicitly minimize the number of invasive procedures for occult infection detection in chronic pain is novel. It moves beyond general guidelines to create a quantifiable, risk-aware diagnostic pathway, a concept not yet applied in this patient population.

proposals.title: A Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis Framework for Balancing Surgical Site Infection Risk and Procedural Delay in Elective Surgery
proposals.motivation: Pre-operative optimization to reduce infection risk, such as decolonization or glycemic control, often necessitates delaying surgery. This creates a critical clinical dilemma where the benefit of reduced infection risk must be weighed against the harms of delay, including patient deterioration, pain, and system inefficiency. Current guidelines offer population-level recommendations but lack tools for individualized, patient-centered decision-making that incorporates specific risks and preferences.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA) model, incorporating patient-specific risk factors and outcome preferences, can provide a quantifiable, personalized recommendation that more accurately balances the trade-off between surgical site infection (SSI) risk and surgical delay than standard, one-size-fits-all protocols.
proposals.method: We will develop an MCDA model using the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) to structure the decision problem. Key criteria (e.g., SSI probability, delay-associated morbidity, patient anxiety, healthcare costs) will be weighted through expert elicitation with surgeons and infectious disease specialists. Patient-specific SSI risk will be calculated using a validated pre-operative risk index (e.g., NNIS risk index). The model will be implemented as a web-based decision support tool, and its output will be retrospectively validated against a cohort of historical elective surgery cases where delay decisions were made, comparing model recommendations to actual outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, operationalized framework for personalized infection prevention decision-making in surgery. It will directly translate operations research methodologies into clinical practice, moving beyond population-level guidelines. The resulting tool has the potential to improve patient-centered care by making the trade-offs in perioperative management explicit and quantifiable.
proposals.novelty: While MCDA has been used in healthcare policy, its application to real-time, bedside clinical decisions in infection prevention is novel. This approach moves from a binary 'delay or not' paradigm to a nuanced, preference-sensitive model that integrates operations research directly into the workflow of surgical and critical care teams.

proposals.title: A Preoperative Vaccine Acceptance Intervention: Adapting Public Health Communication Strategies to Improve Uptake of Recommended Vaccines in Surgical Patients
proposals.motivation: Preoperative vaccination, particularly for influenza and pneumococcal disease, is a critical yet underutilized strategy for preventing postoperative complications, including ventilator-associated events and hospital-acquired pneumonia. Vaccine hesitancy remains a significant barrier. The preoperative consultation represents a unique, high-impact opportunity for a targeted intervention, as it is a dedicated time for risk-benefit counseling before a major physiologic stressor.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a structured, evidence-based vaccine communication protocol during preoperative counseling, adapted from successful public health interventions, will significantly increase patient acceptance and documented administration of recommended vaccines compared to standard preoperative care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, cluster-randomized trial in a pre-anesthesia evaluation clinic. The intervention arm will utilize a multi-component protocol derived from pre-2026 public health frameworks (e.g., presumptive announcement, motivational interviewing, and structured information on personal benefit). Clinicians will be trained via a standardized module. The primary outcome will be the rate of vaccine administration (influenza/pneumococcal) prior to or on the day of surgery, extracted from the electronic health record. Secondary outcomes will include patient-reported satisfaction and decisional conflict, measured via validated surveys. Analysis will use intention-to-treat principles with mixed-effects models to account for clustering.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide actionable evidence for integrating vaccine promotion into routine perioperative care, directly linking infection prevention strategies to patient-centered communication. It will generate a replicable intervention protocol that bridges public health science and surgical care, with the potential to reduce postoperative infectious morbidity.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Cawcutt's prior work focuses on device- and environment-based prevention strategies, this study novelly applies behavioral science interventions from community settings to the preoperative encounter, creating a human-factors intervention to complement existing technical bundles for pneumonia prevention.

proposals.title: A Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) of the Perioperative Influenza and Pneumococcal Vaccination Pathway to Identify and Prioritize Preventable Failures
proposals.motivation: Despite established guidelines, perioperative vaccination rates for influenza and pneumococcal disease in high-risk surgical patients remain suboptimal, representing a missed opportunity for prevention. This failure increases the risk of postoperative respiratory infections, including nonventilator hospital-acquired pneumonia (NV-HAP), which can lead to prolonged hospitalization, ventilator use, and increased morbidity. A systematic, proactive risk assessment of the entire vaccination pathway is needed to move beyond reactive strategies and design reliable, patient-centered prevention systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a structured healthcare FMEA to the perioperative vaccination pathway will identify high-risk, previously uncharacterized failure modes (e.g., in screening, ordering, patient education, or administration) and that targeted, multi-component safeguards based on this analysis will be feasible and prioritized by clinical stakeholders for implementation.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-disciplinary FMEA with a team of surgeons, anesthesiologists, nurses, pharmacists, and infection preventionists. The team will first map the detailed perioperative vaccination pathway from preoperative assessment to post-discharge follow-up. For each step, the team will identify potential failure modes, their causes, and effects, then numerically score them for severity, occurrence, and detection (pre-2026 standard FMEA scales). Risk Priority Numbers (RPNs) will be calculated (RPN = S x O x D) to rank failures. High-priority failures (e.g., RPN > 125) will be used to co-design specific, actionable safeguards with the team.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, prioritized list of actionable failure points within a critical prevention pathway, directly informing quality improvement initiatives. It will extend the application of proactive engineering safety models, commonly used for device or ventilator-associated event prevention, to the domain of perioperative immunizations. The resulting framework can be adapted for other preventive care bundles in acute care settings.
proposals.novelty: While FMEA has been applied to medication administration and device use, its targeted application to the complex, multi-step process of perioperative vaccination is novel. This approach moves beyond documenting low vaccination rates to systematically deconstruct the 'why' and prioritize interventions, integrating Dr. Cawcutt's expertise in prevention strategies and infection impact into a new methodological domain.

proposals.title: Unmasking Latent Immunodeficiency: A Case-Control Study of Rare Mold Infections in Presumed Immunocompetent ICU Patients
proposals.motivation: Invasive mold infections in patients classified as immunocompetent present a significant clinical challenge, leading to delayed diagnosis, poor outcomes, and high mortality. Current infection prevention strategies, including those for ventilator-associated events, are predicated on standard host defense models, potentially overlooking subtle immune deficits. Identifying these deficits is critical for developing targeted prevention and care strategies for these high-risk infections.
proposals.hypothesis: Patients admitted to the ICU with invasive mold infections (e.g., mucormycosis, fusariosis) who lack classic immunocompromising conditions harbor specific, identifiable deficits in innate or cellular immunity that predispose them to infection.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, matched case-control study in a large academic ICU (2018-2024). Cases are adults with proven/probable invasive mold infection without traditional immunosuppression. Controls (1:2) are matched by age, sex, and primary diagnosis. Using stored serum and peripheral blood mononuclear cells, we will perform a multi-analyte panel (Luminex) to quantify cytokines (e.g., IFN-γ, IL-17, GM-CSF) and perform functional immune assays (neutrophil oxidative burst, lymphocyte stimulation). Machine learning (supervised: random forest; unsupervised: hierarchical clustering) will identify immune signature patterns distinguishing cases from controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a novel immunological phenotype of 'at-risk' hosts, moving beyond the binary label of immunocompetence. It will directly inform surveillance and prophylactic strategies for mold infections in the ICU. The findings could lead to a bedside risk stratification tool, aligning with Dr. Cawcutt's focus on preventive care and infection impact.
proposals.novelty: This study shifts the paradigm from classifying patients based on the absence of overt immunosuppression to actively profiling for specific immune vulnerabilities. It integrates detailed immune phenotyping with machine learning in an ICU population, a novel approach not applied to this specific clinical question pre-2026.

proposals.title: Temporal Dynamics and Primary Pathogen Role in Respiratory Co-Infections: A Prospective Cohort Study in Mechanically Ventilated Patients
proposals.motivation: The clinical paradigm for ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) and ventilator-associated events (VAEs) often assumes bacterial infection is secondary to a preceding viral illness or lung injury. This assumption guides antimicrobial stewardship and prevention strategies, such as those in the 2022 SHEA/IDSA/APIC guidelines. However, this may overlook scenarios where bacterial colonization or low-grade infection is primary, with viral superinfection occurring secondarily, potentially misdirecting empirical therapy and infection control efforts.
proposals.hypothesis: In a subset of patients with microbiologically confirmed respiratory viral-bacterial co-infection, the bacterial pathogen is the primary driver of the clinical syndrome, with viral detection representing secondary acquisition or reactivation.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective observational cohort study of mechanically ventilated adults in ICUs who develop a VAE. Using comprehensive molecular testing (multiplex PCR panels for respiratory viruses and bacteria) on serial lower respiratory tract samples, we will establish precise timelines of pathogen detection. We will employ causal inference modeling, specifically a targeted maximum likelihood estimation (TMLE) framework, to analyze the temporal sequence and microbial burden data, adjusting for host factors (immunosuppression, antibiotics) and clinical markers (procalcitonin, host transcriptional signatures) to infer the most likely primary pathogen.
proposals.contribution: This work will challenge the traditional secondary infection model by providing data-driven evidence on the primary etiology of co-infections. It will directly inform more precise diagnostic algorithms and targeted prevention strategies, such as refining the timing of bioburden reduction interventions, potentially improving antimicrobial use and patient outcomes in critical care.
proposals.novelty: While prior research focuses on preventing secondary bacterial infections, this study systematically inverts the question to investigate primary bacterial drivers, applying causal inference methods to microbial temporal data—an approach not yet standard in hospital-acquired infection research.

proposals.title: A Clinical-Microbiological Scoring System to Differentiate Causative from Incidental Viral Pathogens in Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia
proposals.motivation: The widespread use of multiplex molecular panels and metagenomic sequencing in acute-care hospitals has increased the detection of respiratory viruses in patients with pneumonia, including ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP). However, distinguishing whether a detected virus is the causative pathogen or an incidental finding (e.g., prolonged shedding, colonization) remains a critical clinical dilemma, leading to potential antimicrobial overuse and misdirected infection prevention strategies. This ambiguity complicates patient care and hampers accurate outcome measurement for quality improvement initiatives.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a composite score integrating clinical, radiographic, laboratory, and microbiological data points available within the first 48 hours of diagnosis can reliably differentiate viral pathogens causing VAP from incidental detections.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, retrospective cohort study of adult ICU patients with VAP and a respiratory virus detected via PCR or panel testing from 2020-2024. Using expert adjudication by a panel of intensivists and infectious diseases physicians as the reference standard, we will perform multivariate logistic regression to identify independent predictors of viral causation. Predictors will include temporal sequence of symptoms and detection, quantitative viral load (Ct values), presence of a compatible radiographic infiltrate, leukocyte count, absence of alternative bacterial/fungal pathogens, and host immune status. The resulting model will be transformed into a practical clinical scoring algorithm (e.g., the VIRAL-CAUSE score).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide clinicians with a validated, evidence-based tool to improve the diagnostic specificity of viral VAP, guiding more appropriate antiviral use and targeted infection prevention bundles. It will also advance the field by creating a standardized research definition for viral-associated VAP, enabling more accurate epidemiological studies and outcome comparisons.
proposals.novelty: While prior work, including the 2022 VAP prevention strategies, focuses on bacterial pathogens, this proposal directly addresses the growing diagnostic challenge posed by advanced viral diagnostics. It moves beyond simple detection to causal attribution by synthesizing pre-2026 clinical and laboratory data into a novel, actionable scoring system.

proposals.title: A Multicenter, Prospective Cohort Study of Bacterial-Fungal Co-infections in Ventilated Patients: Epidemiology, Impact, and Preventative Care Strategies
proposals.motivation: Co-infections, particularly bacterial-fungal combinations in ventilator-associated events (VAEs), are increasingly recognized but poorly understood. Pre-2026, most data come from single-center, retrospective studies prone to bias and limited generalizability, hindering the development of effective prevention and management strategies. This knowledge gap directly impacts patient care and outcomes in critical care settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that bacterial-fungal co-infections in patients with ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) are associated with distinct clinical risk profiles, worse clinical outcomes (e.g., prolonged ventilation, mortality), and are potentially preventable through targeted, bundled care strategies.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 24-month prospective, observational cohort study across 10-12 geographically diverse academic and community acute-care hospitals. Consecutive adult patients mechanically ventilated >48 hours will be enrolled. Standardized, protocol-driven surveillance for VAEs/VAP will be performed with comprehensive microbiological sampling. Co-infection will be rigorously defined as the isolation of ≥1 bacterial and ≥1 fungal pathogen from lower respiratory tract specimens meeting quantitative thresholds within a 48-hour window. Multivariable logistic regression and competing risks survival analysis will be used to identify independent risk factors and quantify the attributable impact on outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first robust, multicenter prospective data on the incidence, microbiology, and attributable burden of bacterial-fungal respiratory co-infections in ventilated patients. It will generate evidence to refine existing VAP prevention bundles and inform the design of future interventional trials targeting co-infection risk reduction.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond single-center, retrospective analyses of monomicrobial infections to prospectively characterize the complex epidemiology of co-infections using a standardized, multi-institutional framework aligned with contemporary CDC VAE surveillance paradigms.

proposals.title: Ecological Network Analysis of Viral-Bacterial Interactions in the Ventilated Lung: A Novel Framework for Predicting and Preventing Pathogen Co-colonization
proposals.motivation: Ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) and ventilator-associated events (VAEs) remain significant causes of morbidity and mortality in critically ill patients. Pre-2026 evidence indicates that viral infections, such as influenza and SARS-CoV-2, can predispose patients to secondary bacterial pneumonia through complex, poorly understood ecological interactions in the lung microbiome. Understanding these interactions is crucial for developing targeted prevention strategies beyond traditional infection control bundles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific viral-bacterial pathogen pairs exhibit non-random, cooperative network structures within the lower respiratory tract of mechanically ventilated patients, and that the topology of these interaction networks can predict the risk of subsequent VAP/VAE.
proposals.method: We will perform a prospective cohort study of mechanically ventilated patients, collecting serial lower respiratory tract samples via mini-bronchoalveolar lavage for metagenomic sequencing. Using pre-2026 ecological network analysis methods, we will construct co-occurrence and co-exclusion networks from microbial abundance data. We will apply the SPIEC-EASI (Sparse Inverse Covariance Estimation for Ecological Association Inference) algorithm to infer robust microbial interactions, and use the igraph package in R to calculate network metrics (e.g., centrality, modularity) for key viral and bacterial nodes. These network features will be integrated into a multivariate Cox proportional hazards model to predict VAP/VAE development.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first ecological network map of viral-bacterial interactions in the ventilated human lung, moving beyond single-pathogen paradigms. It will identify high-risk pathogen pairs and network signatures that precede clinical infection, offering novel biomarkers for risk stratification. The findings will directly inform the development of next-generation, ecology-informed prevention strategies, such as targeted antimicrobial or immunomodulatory approaches.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Cawcutt's prior work has focused on discrete prevention strategies for device-associated infections, this proposal applies principles from community ecology and network science—methods not yet widely used in clinical VAP research—to model the dynamic, multi-kingdom microbial interactions that underlie infection risk.

proposals.title: Causal Inference Analysis of Viral-Bacterial Interactions in Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) remains a leading cause of morbidity and mortality in critical care. Observational studies suggest viral detection often precedes bacterial pneumonia, but it is unclear if this is a causal relationship or merely an association due to shared risk factors. Establishing causality is crucial for developing targeted prevention strategies, such as antiviral prophylaxis or enhanced surveillance in patients with viral respiratory infections.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the detection of a respiratory virus (e.g., influenza, RSV, rhinovirus) in a mechanically ventilated patient is a causal risk factor for the subsequent development of bacterial VAP, independent of baseline patient severity and other confounding factors.
proposals.method: We will apply a target trial emulation framework to retrospective electronic health record data from adult ICU patients. The exposure is viral detection via PCR within 48 hours of intubation. The primary outcome is bacterial VAP, confirmed by quantitative culture. We will use propensity score matching to balance measured confounders (e.g., age, APACHE II, comorbidities, immunosuppression) between exposed and unexposed patients. The primary causal effect will be estimated using a marginal structural model with inverse probability of treatment weighting to account for time-varying confounders like antibiotic use and sedation levels.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a rigorous, causal estimate of the effect of viral infection on bacterial VAP risk, moving beyond correlation. It will directly inform whether prevention strategies should target viral-bacterial interactions. The application of advanced epidemiologic causal methods to this specific ICU problem represents a methodological advancement for infection prevention research.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Cawcutt's prior work has focused on descriptive epidemiology and bundled prevention strategies for VAP, this study uniquely applies formal causal inference frameworks (target trial emulation, marginal structural models) to disentangle the specific etiologic role of viruses, a question not addressable by traditional observational studies.

proposals.title: Metagenomic Sequencing to Characterize Polymicrobial Co-Infections in Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia: A Pilot Study
proposals.motivation: Ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) remains a leading cause of morbidity and mortality in critically ill patients. Standard culture-based diagnostics often fail to detect the full spectrum of co-infecting pathogens, including fastidious bacteria, viruses, and fungi, which can lead to inappropriate antimicrobial therapy and poor outcomes. A comprehensive, culture-independent approach is needed to elucidate the true microbial complexity of VAP and inform targeted prevention and treatment strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying metagenomic next-generation sequencing (mNGS) and established microbiome analysis pipelines to bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) fluid will identify a greater diversity of known and unexpected co-infecting pathogens in VAP compared to standard clinical microbiology cultures.
proposals.method: We will perform shotgun metagenomic sequencing on BAL samples from 50 patients with clinically diagnosed VAP. Raw sequencing reads will be processed using the QIIME 2 (version 2024.11) pipeline for quality control, adapter trimming, and host (human) sequence depletion. Taxonomic profiling will be performed using Kraken2/Bracken against curated genomic databases (RefSeq). Functional potential will be assessed via HUMAnN 3.0 for pathway analysis. Results will be compared to paired standard-of-care microbiological culture data.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a foundational characterization of the polymicrobial landscape of VAP using mNGS, identifying potential targets for novel diagnostic panels. It will generate pilot data on the prevalence and clinical relevance of co-infections and antimicrobial resistance genes missed by culture, directly informing future interventional studies on pathogen-directed prevention and care bundles.
proposals.novelty: While microbiome pipelines are standard in environmental and gut research, their rigorous application to the acute, high-stakes context of VAP co-infections is novel. This work adapts these robust bioinformatic tools to address a critical gap in hospital-acquired infection diagnostics, moving beyond single-pathogen detection to a syndromic, multi-kingdom assessment.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning Approach to Predict Mortality and Ventilator Duration in ICU Patients with Polymicrobial Ventilator-Associated Infections
proposals.motivation: Ventilator-associated infections (VAIs), including pneumonia and events, remain a leading cause of morbidity and mortality in critically ill patients. Co-infections with multiple pathogens are common and create complex clinical profiles, but current risk stratification is limited. Predicting outcomes based on specific pathogen combinations could enable more personalized, pre-emptive care and resource allocation.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning models trained on detailed microbiological and clinical data can accurately predict 30-day mortality and prolonged mechanical ventilation (>14 days) in patients with polymicrobial VAIs, outperforming traditional severity scores.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from adult ICU patients with polymicrobial VAIs (2018-2024). Features will include pathogen identities (from culture/NGS), antimicrobial resistance profiles, timing, and key clinical variables. We will implement and compare several pre-2026 ML algorithms, including Random Forest, Gradient Boosting Machines (XGBoost), and a basic feed-forward neural network, using nested cross-validation for robust performance estimation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated predictive tool for a high-risk patient subgroup, directly informing bedside clinical decision-making. It will also identify which pathogen combinations and resistance patterns are most strongly associated with poor outcomes, offering new biological insights for targeted prevention strategies.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been applied to predict sepsis or VAE occurrence, its use for outcome prediction specifically in the context of defined polymicrobial infection profiles is novel. This approach moves beyond single-pathogen models to address the real-world complexity of ICU co-infections.

proposals.title: Global, Longitudinal Surveillance of Ventilator-Associated Co-Infections: Epidemiology, Resistance Evolution, and Impact on Patient Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Ventilator-associated events (VAEs), including pneumonia (VAP), remain a leading cause of morbidity and mortality in ICUs globally. Pre-2026, surveillance and prevention strategies are often based on single-center or national data, lacking a comprehensive understanding of the global epidemiology of co-infections (e.g., bacterial-fungal or polymicrobial infections) and the longitudinal evolution of antimicrobial resistance (AMR) patterns, which are critical for effective empiric therapy and infection prevention bundle design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the epidemiology of ventilator-associated co-infections and the evolutionary trajectory of associated AMR exhibit significant geographic and seasonal variation, and that specific pathogen pairs or clusters are independently associated with increased mortality and ventilator duration.
proposals.method: We propose a prospective, multi-year cohort study across a network of 50-100 acute-care hospitals on 6 continents, enrolling mechanically ventilated patients meeting VAE criteria. Standardized protocols will be used for microbiological sampling, identification (including MALDI-TOF MS), and antimicrobial susceptibility testing (AST). Data on clinical outcomes, prevention bundle compliance, and antimicrobial use will be collected. Analysis will employ time-series algorithms to model seasonal trends, geospatial mapping for regional patterns, and multivariable Cox regression and machine learning (e.g., random forest) models to identify co-infection clusters impacting outcomes and to track AMR evolution over time.
proposals.contribution: This study will generate the first truly global, longitudinal dataset on VAE co-infections, defining baseline rates, regional hotspots, and seasonal dynamics. It will directly inform the refinement of international prevention guidelines (e.g., SHEA/IDSA) by identifying high-risk pathogen combinations and provide an early warning system for emerging resistance patterns, guiding empirical antibiotic stewardship in critical care.
