proposals.novelty: While prior work, including the 2022 update on prevention strategies, synthesizes existing evidence, this proposal moves from synthesis to generating novel, large-scale, prospective surveillance data focused explicitly on co-infection dynamics and AMR evolution on a global scale over multiple years, a gap in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Dissecting Viral-Bacterial Synergy in Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia: A Murine Model to Elucidate Immune Modulation and Epithelial Barrier Dysfunction
proposals.motivation: Ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) remains a leading cause of morbidity and mortality in critically ill patients, with viral-bacterial co-infections posing a particularly severe clinical challenge. Pre-2026 clinical and epidemiological studies suggest synergistic interactions, especially between influenza virus and Staphylococcus aureus or Pseudomonas aeruginosa, worsen outcomes, but the precise molecular and immunological mechanisms driving this synergy in the context of mechanical ventilation are poorly understood. This knowledge gap limits the development of targeted preventive and therapeutic strategies beyond broad-spectrum antimicrobials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that preceding viral infection in mechanically ventilated hosts induces specific, time-dependent dysregulation of alveolar macrophage function and epithelial tight junctions, creating a permissive niche for enhanced bacterial adhesion, invasion, and evasion of early innate immune clearance.
proposals.method: We will employ a well-established murine model of orotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation. Mice will be inoculated with influenza A virus (H1N1 PR8 strain) followed 3-7 days later by bacterial challenge with clinical isolates of methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) or Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Control groups will receive virus alone, bacteria alone, or sham infection. We will use flow cytometry to quantify and phenotype alveolar macrophages (CD11c+, Siglec-F+) and neutrophils in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid, assessing surface markers (e.g., MARCO, TLRs) and intracellular cytokine production. Epithelial barrier integrity will be assessed via measurement of transepithelial electrical resistance in primary cell cultures and immunohistochemistry for tight junction proteins (ZO-1, occludin) in lung sections. Bacterial burden, inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, IFN-γ), and lung histopathology will be correlated with immune profiling data.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond association to define the causal immunological and epithelial mechanisms of viral-bacterial synergy in a controlled, ventilated system. It will identify potential host-directed therapeutic targets (e.g., specific macrophage pathways, barrier-stabilizing agents) to prevent secondary bacterial pneumonia. The findings will provide a mechanistic foundation for refining VAP prevention bundles, potentially informing the timing or selection of immunomodulatory adjuncts to standard care.
proposals.novelty: While prior animal studies have examined viral-bacterial interactions, few have integrated the critical variable of mechanical ventilation, which itself alters lung immunity and injury. This model uniquely combines precise viral priming, clinically relevant bacterial pathogens, and the ventilated host environment to dissect mechanisms most pertinent to the ICU setting where VAP occurs.

proposals.title: Correlation Between SARS-CoV-2 Cycle Threshold Values and Viral Culture Positivity in Critically Ill Patients: A Prospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Cycle threshold (Ct) values from PCR tests are frequently used to guide infection prevention decisions, including isolation precautions, based on the assumption that high Ct values indicate low or non-existent infectivity. This assumption is particularly critical in acute-care settings like ICUs, where vulnerable patients are at high risk for nosocomial infections. However, pre-2026 evidence suggests viral culture positivity, a better proxy for infectivity, can persist at high Ct values in certain populations, potentially rendering current IPC strategies inadequate.
proposals.hypothesis: In critically ill patients, SARS-CoV-2 PCR Ct value is a poor predictor of viral culture positivity, and the relationship between Ct and infectivity is significantly modified by host factors such as immunosuppression and severity of illness.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of PCR-positive COVID-19 patients admitted to the ICU. For each patient, we will collect serial respiratory samples paired with clinical metadata. All samples will undergo standardized viral culture in Vero E6 cells. We will use multivariable logistic regression with viral culture positivity as the primary outcome and Ct value as the main predictor, adjusting for and testing interactions with key covariates (e.g., immunocompromised status, days since symptom onset, use of corticosteroids). Receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis will determine the optimal Ct threshold for predicting culture negativity in this population.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide direct, clinical evidence on the reliability of Ct values for assessing transmission risk in a high-stakes ICU environment. The findings will inform more nuanced, patient-specific infection prevention protocols, potentially leading to updates in guidelines for isolation and contact precautions. It directly extends Dr. Cawcutt's work on preventing ventilator-associated events by addressing a key diagnostic uncertainty that impacts cohorting and resource allocation.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have examined Ct-culture correlation in general populations, this focuses specifically on the critically ill—a group with altered viral dynamics where guideline assumptions may fail most dramatically. It integrates detailed host-factor analysis into the model of infectivity, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all Ct threshold.

proposals.title: A Pragmatic, Multi-Center Study to Develop and Validate Standardized Ct Value Reporting Protocols for Guiding Respiratory Isolation Decisions
proposals.motivation: Cycle threshold (Ct) values from PCR tests for respiratory pathogens like SARS-CoV-2 and influenza offer a potential proxy for infectiousness, but their use in infection control decisions is inconsistent and unstandardized. This lack of standardization leads to variable isolation practices, potentially increasing transmission risk or unnecessarily prolonging isolation, which consumes resources and impacts patient care. A standardized, evidence-based protocol is urgently needed to translate Ct data into reliable, actionable guidance for healthcare workers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing a standardized, pathogen-specific Ct value reporting protocol, coupled with clinician education, will reduce inter-hospital variability in isolation decisions and improve the appropriateness of isolation duration compared to current practice.
proposals.method: This multi-phase study will first convene a Delphi panel of infectious diseases and infection prevention experts to establish consensus on pathogen-specific Ct thresholds for discontinosing isolation for key respiratory viruses. This protocol will then be implemented in a pragmatic, stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial across 10-12 acute-care hospitals. The intervention includes integrating the Ct thresholds into electronic health record reporting and a targeted education module for critical care and infection prevention staff. The primary outcome will be the coefficient of variation in isolation duration for the same pathogen and Ct range across hospitals pre- and post-intervention, analyzed using mixed-effects regression models.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first consensus-driven, operational protocol for using Ct values in infection control decisions, directly addressing a critical gap in pandemic and seasonal respiratory virus management. It will provide actionable data on the feasibility and impact of standardizing this biomarker to make isolation practices more precise, efficient, and consistent, thereby enhancing patient safety and resource utilization.
proposals.novelty: While existing guidelines discuss Ct values in theory, no study has yet developed and tested a specific, implementable reporting protocol across multiple hospitals to standardize real-world clinical decisions. This moves beyond descriptive analysis to a pragmatic intervention trial in the complex environment of acute-care hospitals.

proposals.title: A Clinical-Decision Algorithm Integrating Quantitative PCR Cycle Threshold Values and Clinical Markers to Guide Antibiotic De-escalation in Ventilated Patients with Pneumonia
proposals.motivation: In ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) and non-ventilator hospital-acquired pneumonia (NV-HAP), determining the optimal duration of antibiotic therapy is challenging. While quantitative PCR Ct values correlate with bacterial load, they are not used in isolation for clinical decisions. Premature antibiotic cessation risks treatment failure, while prolonged therapy drives antimicrobial resistance and adverse events, highlighting a critical need for more precise stewardship tools.
proposals.hypothesis: A decision algorithm that integrates serial quantitative Ct values from respiratory pathogen PCR panels with specific, validated clinical improvement markers (e.g., Clinical Pulmonary Infection Score [CPIS] components, ventilator liberation parameters) will identify patients suitable for safe antibiotic de-escalation or discontinuation earlier than standard clinical assessment alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational cohort study in adult ICUs. For patients diagnosed with pneumonia (VAP or NV-HAP), we will collect daily clinical data (CPIS, oxygenation, vasopressor need, temperature, white blood cell count) and serial respiratory PCR Ct values for the causative pathogen(s). Using classification and regression tree (CART) analysis, we will develop an algorithm to predict clinical stability. The algorithm's performance for guiding a simulated 48-hour early antibiotic stop will be compared against a clinician-managed control cohort using propensity score matching, with the primary outcome being a composite of treatment failure and antibiotic-free days.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a structured, data-driven framework to enhance antimicrobial stewardship in pneumonia care. It directly addresses the 2022 SHEA/IDSA/APIC strategies to prevent healthcare-associated pneumonia by offering a novel tool for optimizing therapy duration. The validated algorithm could be integrated into electronic health records to provide real-time, patient-specific decision support.
proposals.novelty: While Ct values are recognized, no pre-2026 algorithm formally combines dynamic Ct trends with granular, daily clinical status markers to create a actionable de-escalation rule for pneumonia. This moves beyond using Ct as a standalone diagnostic adjunct to integrating it into a longitudinal treatment pathway.

proposals.title: Modeling SARS-CoV-2 Viral Kinetics to Predict Clearance and Inform Infection Prevention Strategies in Critically Ill Patients
proposals.motivation: Despite established prevention bundles, SARS-CoV-2 transmission in healthcare settings, particularly among critically ill patients, remains a significant challenge. Current infection control decisions, such as isolation duration, are often based on fixed timeframes or qualitative PCR results rather than quantitative, patient-specific infectivity risk. Applying established quantitative viral dynamic models from HIV research offers a novel, data-driven approach to predict infectiousness and optimize prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a target-cell limited model with an immune response component, adapted from HIV viral dynamics, can accurately fit longitudinal SARS-CoV-2 viral load data from critically ill patients and predict the time to viral clearance below a transmission risk threshold.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of mechanically ventilated patients with SARS-CoV-2, extracting serial cycle threshold (Ct) values from clinical PCR tests. We will adapt a two-phase viral dynamic model, commonly used in HIV, parameterized for SARS-CoV-2 replication and clearance. Using nonlinear mixed-effects modeling (NONMEM software), we will fit patient-level viral load trajectories to estimate key parameters: the infected cell clearance rate (δ) and the viral clearance rate (c). Model-predicted time to clearance below a Ct value of 30 (a pre-2026 surrogate for low culturable virus) will be compared to observed data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for predicting SARS-CoV-2 infectivity decay in complex critically ill patients, moving beyond one-size-fits-all isolation protocols. It will directly inform infection prevention strategies by offering a model to tailor isolation and contact precautions based on individual viral kinetics, potentially reducing unnecessary resource use and optimizing patient care.
proposals.novelty: While viral kinetics models are standard in HIV, their application to inform real-time infection prevention decisions for acute respiratory viruses in the ICU is novel. This bridges the fields of quantitative virology and practical hospital epidemiology, aligning with Dr. Cawcutt's expertise in preventing ventilator-associated events through novel, measurable interventions.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Net Benefit of Cycle Threshold Values in Guiding Antibiotic Stewardship for Suspected Ventilator-Associated Infections: A Decision Curve Analysis
proposals.motivation: In critical care, differentiating colonization from true infection in ventilator-associated conditions is challenging. Over-reliance on PCR cycle threshold (Ct) values, which correlate with microbial load, can lead to unnecessary antibiotic use, driving resistance and patient harm. A formal assessment of whether Ct values improve upon clinical judgment alone for antibiotic decisions is urgently needed to inform stewardship strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating quantitative PCR Ct values into the diagnostic evaluation of suspected lower respiratory tract infections in ventilated patients will provide a net benefit over clinical judgment alone across a range of clinically reasonable threshold probabilities for treating.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective diagnostic study in adult ICU patients with suspected ventilator-associated pneumonia or events. For each episode, clinicians will pre-specify their pre-test probability of a bacterial infection requiring treatment (clinical judgment). We will obtain quantitative PCR Ct values for common pathogens from endotracheal aspirates. The reference standard will be a composite of quantitative culture and expert adjudication. Decision curve analysis will be performed to compare the net benefit of three strategies: 'treat all', 'treat based on clinical judgment alone', and 'treat based on a model combining clinical judgment and Ct values'.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a quantitative, clinically interpretable metric (net benefit) for evaluating the utility of Ct values in a critical stewardship decision. It will move beyond traditional accuracy metrics to inform whether and when this diagnostic technology should be integrated into care pathways to optimize antibiotic use and prevent harm.
proposals.novelty: While Ct values are widely available, their clinical utility for stewardship decisions is debated. Applying decision curve analysis to this problem is novel, as prior work focuses on diagnostic accuracy rather than the net consequence of using the test to guide treatment in the context of ventilator-associated events.

proposals.title: Statistical Process Control for Real-Time Outbreak Detection: A Pilot Study Using SARS-CoV-2 Cycle Threshold Value Trends in a Critical Care Unit
proposals.motivation: Hospital-acquired infections, including ventilator-associated events, remain a persistent source of patient morbidity and mortality. Traditional outbreak detection relies on lagging indicators like confirmed infection counts, delaying intervention. Real-time, data-driven methods are needed to flag atypical transmission events earlier, particularly in high-risk settings like ICUs.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying statistical process control (SPC) charts, specifically individual moving range (I-MR) and cumulative sum (CUSUM) control charts, to trends in pathogen-specific PCR cycle threshold (Ct) values will enable earlier detection of atypical case clusters compared to standard surveillance based on infection counts alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study in a single academic ICU over 24 months. For all patients tested for SARS-CoV-2 via a standardized PCR assay, daily median Ct values will be calculated and plotted on an I-MR chart to monitor process stability. A CUSUM chart will be implemented in parallel to detect small, sustained shifts in Ct values, which may indicate an outbreak of genetically similar strains. Control limits will be established using a baseline period, with signals triggering a formal infection prevention review.
proposals.contribution: This work will translate a proven industrial quality control method into a novel biosurveillance tool for infection prevention. It will provide a framework for using routinely available molecular diagnostic data (Ct values) for real-time outbreak management, potentially shortening the time to intervention. The study will generate pilot data on the sensitivity and specificity of SPC for this application.
proposals.novelty: While SPC is established in healthcare for monitoring process metrics, its direct application to continuous molecular diagnostic data (Ct values) for outbreak detection is novel. This approach moves beyond monitoring infection rates to analyzing the underlying microbiological data for early signal detection.

proposals.title: A Pragmatic Clinical Trial of Ct-Value-Guided, Real-Time Isolation Decisions Using Point-of-Care PCR for Suspected Respiratory Pathogens in the ICU
proposals.motivation: Current infection prevention strategies for ventilator-associated events rely on syndromic surveillance and culture-based diagnostics, which are slow and lack specificity. This leads to prolonged, unnecessary isolation for patients with low bioburden or non-infectious syndromes, consuming resources and potentially harming patient care. Rapid, bedside PCR tests are now available, but their quantitative Cycle threshold (Ct) values, indicative of pathogen load, are not systematically used to inform real-time isolation decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a protocol that uses rapid PCR Ct values to guide isolation decisions for suspected respiratory pathogens will reduce unnecessary isolation time without increasing healthcare-associated transmission events, compared to standard of care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial in four adult ICUs. For patients triggering a clinical suspicion for a respiratory pathogen, a nasopharyngeal swab will be tested using the Cepheid Xpert Xpress Flu/RSV/SARS-CoV-2 or BioFire FilmArray Pneumonia panel at the bedside. The intervention protocol will define a Ct-value threshold (e.g., >30-32) above which contact/droplet precautions can be discontinued immediately if clinical suspicion is low. The primary outcome will be hours of unnecessary isolation. Secondary outcomes include transmission events, resource utilization, and nurse compliance assessed via surveys.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first evidence-based protocol for integrating quantitative molecular data into real-time infection prevention practice. It will directly inform guidelines on the operational use of rapid diagnostics to optimize stewardship of isolation resources, potentially improving patient flow and reducing the non-clinical burden on ICU staff.
proposals.novelty: While rapid PCR diagnostics are established, their quantitative Ct values are rarely used dynamically for infection control decisions. This study moves beyond diagnostic accuracy to test a novel, pragmatic application of this existing technology to de-escalate precautions, aligning with precision prevention strategies.

proposals.title: Population-Level Impact of Cycle Threshold-Guided Deisolation Policies on Healthcare-Associated Respiratory Virus Transmission: A Simulation Modeling Study
proposals.motivation: Current infection control policies for respiratory viruses like influenza and SARS-CoV-2 rely on fixed-duration isolation, which can be inefficient, leading to unnecessary bed closures or premature deisolation. Cycle threshold (Ct) values from PCR tests correlate with viral culture positivity and may better indicate infectiousness. However, the system-wide public health impact of implementing Ct-based deisolation rules is unknown, creating a critical evidence gap for scalable, precision-based infection prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a Ct-based deisolation policy for patients with respiratory viral infections will reduce overall healthcare-associated transmission events and patient-days of isolation, compared to standard time-based isolation protocols, when modeled at a healthcare system population level.
proposals.method: We will develop a discrete-event simulation model using a modified susceptible-exposed-infectious-recovered (SEIR) framework within a simulated 10-hospital healthcare system. The model will incorporate patient admission, discharge, and intra-facility transfer data, along with pre-2026 parameters for viral transmission dynamics, PCR Ct value decay, and healthcare worker compliance. The intervention arm will use a pre-specified Ct value (e.g., >30-35) for deisolation, while the control arm uses fixed 5-10 day rules. The primary outcome is the number of healthcare-associated infections averted over one year. Sensitivity analyses will test key parameters using Monte Carlo simulations.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative estimate of the potential benefits and trade-offs of a Ct-guided deisolation strategy at a system scale. It will deliver a decision-support framework for hospital epidemiologists and public health officials considering precision infection control policies, directly extending the 2022 ventilator-associated event prevention strategies to non-ventilator respiratory pathogens.
proposals.novelty: While Ct values have been studied for diagnostic and prognostic use, their application as a dynamic, operational tool for system-wide isolation policy is novel. This moves beyond unit-level bioburden reduction (e.g., mobile phone disinfection) to model a scalable, diagnostic-driven prevention strategy for acute-care hospitals.

proposals.title: Predicting SARS-CoV-2 Infectivity via Electronic Health Record Data: A Machine Learning Approach to Inform Infection Prevention Strategies
proposals.motivation: Cycle threshold (Ct) values from PCR tests correlate with viral load and potential infectivity, but results are often delayed or unavailable for real-time clinical decisions. This gap hinders timely implementation of targeted infection prevention strategies, such as patient cohorting and personal protective equipment allocation, particularly in high-risk settings like ICUs. An accurate, real-time proxy for infectivity derived from routinely collected EHR data could significantly enhance outbreak management and reduce nosocomial transmission.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model trained on pre-2026 clinical and laboratory data from EHRs can accurately predict SARS-CoV-2 PCR Ct values (as a surrogate for infectivity) in hospitalized patients, and that this prediction can be used to classify patients into high vs. low infectivity risk categories.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of patients with confirmed SARS-CoV-2 at a large academic medical center (2020-2024). The model will be trained on structured EHR data: vital signs, routine labs (e.g., CBC, CRP), oxygen requirements, symptom duration, and demographics. The target variable will be the first positive PCR Ct value. We will compare the performance of several pre-2026 algorithms, including XGBoost and a multilayer perceptron neural network, using cross-validation. Model output will be a continuous Ct prediction and a binary classification (e.g., Ct ≤ 30 vs. >30).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-driven tool to estimate patient infectivity in near real-time, directly supporting infection preventionists and frontline clinicians. It translates the concept of 'bioburden reduction' to the digital realm, offering a complementary strategy to physical decolonization. The model could be integrated into clinical decision support systems to guide isolation precautions and resource use, advancing beyond syndromic surveillance.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses EHR data to predict COVID-19 diagnosis or severity, the inverse problem of inferring a specific microbiological quantifier (Ct value) from clinical parameters is novel. This approach moves from diagnosing infection to characterizing transmission risk, a critical shift for infection prevention, especially for ventilator-associated events and other hospital-acquired infections.

proposals.title: Oral Care Efficacy in Mechanically Ventilated Patients: A Prospective Cohort Study of the Modifying Effects of Baseline Oral Microbiome and Periodontal Health
proposals.motivation: Oral care is a cornerstone bundle element for preventing ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) and ventilator-associated events (VAEs), but its efficacy is inconsistently reported across studies. Pre-2026 evidence suggests patient-specific factors, including dysbiosis of the oral microbiome and pre-existing periodontal disease, may influence the risk of respiratory pathogen colonization and subsequent pneumonia, potentially explaining variable intervention outcomes. This study addresses a critical gap in precision infection prevention by investigating whether these factors modify the effectiveness of standardized oral care.
proposals.hypothesis: The efficacy of a standardized oral care intervention in reducing oropharyngeal colonization with respiratory pathogens and incidence of VAP/VAEs is modified by a patient's baseline oral microbiome composition and periodontal health status.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational cohort study of 200 mechanically ventilated adults in the ICU. At intubation, we will collect oral swabs for 16S rRNA gene sequencing to characterize baseline microbiome (analyzing alpha/beta diversity and specific taxa via QIIME2) and perform a standardized periodontal assessment. All patients will receive a protocolized, twice-daily chlorhexidine-based oral care bundle. Primary outcomes are quantitative changes in pathogen burden (via serial qPCR for S. aureus, P. aeruginosa, and Enterobacterales) and VAP/VAE rates. We will use multivariable logistic regression and interaction term analysis to test for effect modification by microbiome clusters and periodontal score.
proposals.contribution: This work will move infection prevention beyond a one-size-fits-all approach by identifying patient subgroups who derive maximal benefit from oral care. It will provide a biological rationale for heterogeneous trial results and generate pilot data for future stratified or adjunctive intervention trials targeting high-risk microbiomes.
proposals.novelty: While prior research links oral dysbiosis to VAP risk, no pre-2026 study has directly tested the oral microbiome as an effect modifier of a preventive intervention's efficacy. Integrating detailed microbial ecology with clinical periodontal assessment to personalize VAP prevention strategies is a novel, mechanism-driven approach.

proposals.title: Mapping and Targeting Environmental Pathogen Reservoirs to Prevent Ventilator-Associated Events: A Prospective, Multi-Modal Intervention Study
proposals.motivation: Despite robust patient-focused prevention bundles, ventilator-associated events (VAEs) persist, causing significant morbidity and mortality. Pre-2026 evidence increasingly implicates the inanimate hospital environment as a persistent reservoir for multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs) that seed infections. A paradigm shift from solely patient-centric interventions to include direct disruption of these environmental reservoirs is needed to break the transmission cycle.
proposals.hypothesis: A multimodal intervention targeting high-touch environmental reservoirs in ICU rooms, with a specific focus on pathogen bioburden on ventilator surfaces and associated equipment, will reduce the incidence of ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) and VAEs compared to standard prevention bundles alone.
proposals.method: This prospective, controlled study will employ a before-and-after design in medical and surgical ICUs. The intervention will integrate three components: 1) Systematic environmental mapping using adenosine triphosphate (ATP) bioluminescence and targeted microbial cultures to identify high-burden reservoirs on ventilators, monitors, and workstations. 2) A structured, enhanced disinfection protocol for identified high-risk surfaces using an EPA-approved disinfectant with proven efficacy against MDROs. 3) A behavioral nudge strategy for staff, using audit and feedback of ATP results. The primary outcome will be the incidence density of VAP/VAE, analyzed using interrupted time-series analysis.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence for the efficacy of a pathogen-reservoir-focused prevention strategy. It will generate a replicable model for environmental bioburden mapping in critical care and quantify the additive benefit of environmental disruption to existing patient care bundles, potentially offering a new standard for comprehensive infection prevention.
proposals.novelty: While environmental cleaning is recognized, this study uniquely inverts the focus by systematically mapping pathogen flow *from* specific equipment reservoirs *to* the patient, and tests a targeted, evidence-based disruption protocol integrated into VAE prevention, moving beyond generic cleaning schedules.

proposals.title: Cost-Effectiveness Analysis of a Comprehensive Oral Care Bundle for the Prevention of Nonventilator Hospital-Acquired Pneumonia in Adult Inpatients
proposals.motivation: Nonventilator hospital-acquired pneumonia (NV-HAP) is a common, costly, and morbid healthcare-associated infection, with poor oral hygiene being a key modifiable risk factor. While evidence-based bundles exist for ventilator-associated pneumonia, standardized oral hygiene protocols for the general inpatient population are inconsistently implemented, partly due to perceived resource burdens. A rigorous economic evaluation is needed to inform hospital administrators and infection prevention programs about the potential return on investment of a universal oral care strategy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the implementation of a comprehensive, nurse-driven oral care bundle for all hospitalized patients will be cost-effective, with the reduction in NV-HAP cases and associated costs outweighing the intervention's implementation and maintenance expenses.
proposals.method: We will conduct a quasi-experimental, pre-post study with a concurrent economic evaluation across four adult medical-surgical units in a large academic health system. The intervention is a standardized bundle (toothbrushing twice daily with chlorhexidine or stannous fluoride toothpaste for high-risk patients). Primary outcomes are NV-HAP rates (per 1000 patient-days) and a detailed cost-effectiveness analysis. We will calculate incremental cost-effectiveness ratios (ICERs) from the hospital perspective, incorporating direct costs of the bundle, nursing time (via time-motion study), and costs avoided from prevented NV-HAP (using pre-2026 diagnosis-related group and attributable length-of-stay data). Sensitivity analyses will test parameter uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first detailed economic analyses of a universal oral care bundle, moving beyond efficacy to practical implementation. It will generate a replicable cost model to help hospitals forecast the financial impact of similar programs. The findings will directly support the 2022 strategies to prevent NV-HAP by addressing a critical knowledge gap regarding resource allocation.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Cawcutt's prior work focuses on ventilator-associated events and device-related infections, this study applies a rigorous health economic framework to the understudied problem of NV-HAP prevention in the non-ICU setting, aligning with her theme of preventive strategies for hospital-acquired infections.

proposals.title: A Pragmatic Trial of an Automated VAE Surveillance and Clinical Decision Support System to Reduce Ventilator-Associated Events in the ICU
proposals.motivation: Ventilator-associated events (VAEs) are significant drivers of morbidity, mortality, and cost in critically ill patients. Current surveillance is labor-intensive and retrospective, limiting timely intervention. Integrating automated VAE detection with real-time clinical decision support (CDS) within the electronic health record (EHR) offers a novel strategy to translate surveillance data into immediate, actionable preventive care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing an EHR-embedded system for automated VAE surveillance coupled with real-time, patient-specific CDS prompts will reduce the incidence of VAEs compared to standard, manual surveillance practices.
proposals.method: We will develop a rules-based algorithm using the 2022 NHSN VAE surveillance definitions to analyze EHR data (ventilator settings, oxygenation, temperature, white blood cell count, antimicrobials, radiography reports) in real-time. This algorithm will be integrated into the Epic EHR system to trigger patient-specific CDS alerts (e.g., prompts for sedation vacation, spontaneous breathing trials, oral care, head-of-bed elevation) when a patient meets criteria for a VAE or is at high risk. The intervention will be tested in a stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial across multiple ICUs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, scalable model for automating infection prevention surveillance and directly linking it to care delivery. It will generate high-quality evidence on the clinical impact of real-time, algorithm-driven CDS on VAE rates, moving beyond descriptive surveillance to active prevention.
proposals.novelty: While automated VAE surveillance has been explored, its direct, real-time integration with patient-specific preventive CDS prompts within the clinical workflow is a novel and proactive intervention. This approach operationalizes the 'prevention' strategies from the 2022 guidelines at the point of care.

proposals.title: A Pragmatic, Cluster-Randomized Trial to Evaluate the Impact of a Standardized Early Mobility Protocol on Non-Ventilator Hospital-Acquired Pneumonia (NV-HAP) Incidence in Acute Care Medical Units
proposals.motivation: Non-ventilator hospital-acquired pneumonia (NV-HAP) is a common, costly, and morbid healthcare-associated infection, yet it receives less attention and fewer prevention resources than ventilator-associated pneumonia. Pre-2026 evidence identifies immobility as a key modifiable risk factor for NV-HAP. This study directly addresses a critical gap by rigorously evaluating a targeted, non-device intervention—early patient mobilization—to reduce NV-HAP, aligning with Dr. Cawcutt's expertise in infection prevention strategies for acute care.
proposals.hypothesis: The implementation of a standardized, nurse-driven early mobility protocol will significantly reduce the incidence rate of NV-HAP among non-ICU, acute care medical patients compared to usual care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, cluster-randomized controlled trial across 20 medical units in a multi-hospital system. Units will be randomized to implement a validated, tiered early mobility protocol (intervention) or continue usual care (control). The primary outcome is NV-HAP incidence, defined per 2022 CDC/NHSN surveillance criteria, measured via standardized, blinded adjudication. Secondary outcomes include mobility levels (measured via the Johns Hopkins Highest Level of Mobility scale), length of stay, and cost. Analysis will use an intention-to-treat approach with generalized linear mixed models to account for clustering.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence for the efficacy of early mobility as a primary prevention strategy for NV-HAP, a significant public health burden. It will yield a practical, scalable protocol that integrates infection prevention with routine nursing care, potentially informing future national guideline updates. The findings will directly impact patient safety initiatives by shifting focus toward non-ventilator respiratory infections.
proposals.novelty: While mobility is known to be beneficial, its specific, quantitative impact on NV-HAP rates has not been definitively established in a large, randomized trial. This study moves beyond observational association to test a causal, mechanistic intervention, distinct from device-focused or oral care strategies commonly studied for ventilator-associated pneumonia.

proposals.title: Adaptation and Feasibility of a Scaled-Down Ventilator-Associated Event Prevention Bundle for Ambulatory Surgical Centers
proposals.motivation: Hospital-acquired pneumonia (HAP), including ventilator-associated events (VAEs), is a major source of morbidity, mortality, and cost in inpatient settings. Prevention bundles are well-established for acute-care hospitals but are not designed for resource-limited outpatient environments like ambulatory surgery centers (ASCs), where procedural sedation and post-operative monitoring create infection risk. As surgical volumes shift to ASCs, evidence-based, context-specific prevention strategies are urgently needed to protect patients.
proposals.hypothesis: A scaled-down, multi-modal intervention bundle, adapted from the 2022 SHEA/IDSA/APIC/AHA strategies for VAE prevention, will be feasible to implement and will reduce bioburden and key process failures associated with respiratory infection risk in an ASC setting.
proposals.method: This mixed-methods study will first conduct a Delphi process with a panel of infection preventionists, ASC clinicians, and health services researchers to adapt inpatient VAE bundle elements (e.g., oral care, head-of-bed elevation, sedation vacation, peptic ulcer disease prophylaxis) for ASC constraints. The resulting "ASC-Safe Breath" bundle will be piloted in a single ASC using a pre-post design. Primary outcomes will be feasibility (adherence surveys, time-motion study) and impact on surrogate endpoints (measured via adenosine triphosphate bioluminescence on high-touch respiratory equipment and audit of bundle component compliance), mirroring Dr. Cawcutt's prior work on bioburden reduction.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first pragmatic, ASC-adapted protocol for preventing procedure-associated respiratory infections, filling a critical gap in outpatient infection prevention. It will provide preliminary data on implementation feasibility and impact on bioburden, establishing a foundation for a future multi-center trial on clinical outcomes like non-ventilator HAP.
proposals.novelty: While VAE bundles are standard in ICUs, their systematic adaptation and testing for the unique workflow, staffing, and resource environment of ASCs is novel. This study moves infection prevention science beyond the hospital walls into high-volume outpatient procedural settings.

proposals.title: Optimizing Sedation to Minimize Ventilator-Associated Events: A Dual-Problem Framework for Mechanically Ventilated Patients
proposals.motivation: Ventilator-associated events (VAEs) remain a significant source of morbidity and mortality in critically ill patients, despite established prevention bundles. Concurrently, excessive sedation is a known, modifiable risk factor for prolonged ventilation and VAE development. Current clinical practice often addresses sedation and VAE prevention as separate, sequential problems, missing the opportunity to optimize sedation specifically to reduce VAE risk.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing a protocolized, dual-problem sedation strategy—which explicitly targets minimizing sedation depth while simultaneously incorporating VAE prevention criteria—will significantly reduce the incidence of VAEs compared to standard sedation management.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, cluster-randomized trial in adult ICUs. The intervention arm will employ a dual-problem algorithm: a validated sedation scale (e.g., RASS) will guide titration to a target of light sedation (RASS 0 to -2), while a parallel checklist derived from the 2022 VAE prevention strategies (e.g., daily spontaneous awakening and breathing trials, head-of-bed elevation, oral care) will be integrated into the sedation protocol. The control arm will receive standard, guideline-based sedation. The primary outcome will be VAE rate per 1000 ventilator days, with secondary outcomes including ventilator-free days and opioid/benzodiazepine exposure.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence for a unified clinical protocol that directly links sedation management to VAE prevention. It will generate a practical, algorithmic framework for bedside clinicians to concurrently manage pain/sedation and infection risk, potentially refining future VAE prevention bundles.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in formally framing sedation optimization not just for patient comfort or weaning, but as an integral, algorithmic component of VAE prevention, treating the two goals as a single, dual-objective problem rather than independent tasks.

proposals.title: A Randomized Pilot Trial of Personalized versus Standard Oral Care to Reduce Ventilator-Associated Events in Mechanically Ventilated Patients
proposals.motivation: Nonventilator and ventilator-associated hospital-acquired pneumonias (NV-HAP and VAP) remain significant sources of morbidity, mortality, and cost. Oral care is a cornerstone of prevention, but current guidelines recommend a one-size-fits-all regimen. The oral microbiome is a dynamic reservoir for pathogens, and its composition varies significantly between individuals, suggesting that standardized care may be suboptimal for many high-risk patients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an oral care regimen personalized to a patient's baseline oral microbiome composition and specific clinical risk factors will be more effective at reducing pathogenic oral bioburden and the incidence of ventilator-associated events (VAEs) compared to a standardized oral care protocol.
proposals.method: We will conduct a single-center, pilot randomized controlled trial in a medical ICU. Intubated patients will be randomized to receive either standard chlorhexidine-based oral care or a personalized regimen. For the intervention arm, baseline oral swabs will be analyzed using 16S rRNA gene sequencing. A rule-based algorithm, incorporating microbiome data (e.g., relative abundance of known respiratory pathogens like Pseudomonas or Staphylococcus) and clinical risk factors (e.g., periodontal disease, immunosuppression), will assign one of three pre-defined intensified regimens (e.g., increased frequency, adjunctive topical antimicrobials). Oral microbiome and clinical outcomes will be tracked serially.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first clinical evidence for the feasibility and preliminary efficacy of microbiome-informed oral care in critical illness. It will generate a foundational dataset linking personalized oral care interventions to shifts in the oral microbiome and downstream clinical endpoints, informing the development of future precision prevention strategies for NV-HAP and VAP.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Cawcutt's prior work has focused on optimizing standardized bundles and device disinfection, this proposal moves from a macro, one-size-fits-all strategy to a micro-scale, patient-specific approach. It integrates contemporary microbiome analysis directly into real-time clinical decision-making for infection prevention, a novel application of precision medicine principles in the critical care setting.

ideas.title: Disentangling Viral from Contextual Drivers of Post-Pandemic Chronic Pain: A Stressor-Matched Cohort Study
ideas.description: This study employs a multi-arm prospective cohort design to isolate the specific contribution of SARS-CoV-2 infection to chronic pain development from the contribution of non-infection pandemic-related stressors. It enrolls three groups: individuals with confirmed COVID-19, a stressor-matched control group without infection but with verified exposure to significant pandemic-era psychosocial or economic disruptions, and a pre-pandemic historical control cohort. All participants undergo deep phenotyping at baseline (including detailed stressor inventories, psychosocial assessments, and quantitative sensory testing) and are tracked longitudinally with objective and subjective pain outcome measures. The core analysis compares pain incidence and trajectories between the infected and stressor-matched control groups, controlling for pre-pandemic pain history, to attribute causality specifically to the viral infection.

ideas.title: Home-based immunophenotyping to predict and manage infection-triggered pain in patients receiving long-term IV therapy
ideas.description: This research fuses the study of infection-triggered pain immunology with home-based vascular care protocols to develop a predictive monitoring system. It aims to adapt home IV therapy safety checklists and point-of-care sampling to periodically collect and analyze blood for biomarkers of bystander activation or molecular mimicry following infections. By longitudinally tracking these immune pathways in the home setting, the research seeks to create decision algorithms that can flag early immunological dysregulation, enabling pre-emptive intervention to prevent the transition from acute infection to chronic pain in this vulnerable, home-bound population. This transforms home care from purely procedural safety to include predictive immunologic surveillance.

ideas.title: Home-based precision antimicrobial infusion for chronic pain with infectious history
ideas.description: This research fuses home-based vascular access care with precision criteria for antimicrobial use in chronic pain. It develops integrated decision algorithms that utilize both established clinical biomarkers and real-time, device-collected physiological data (e.g., from smart catheters or connected infusion pumps) to monitor patient response. The synergy enables dynamic, adaptive antimicrobial regimens for chronic pain patients with prior infections, administered safely at home. This shifts treatment from a static, clinic-based decision to a continuous, personalized therapeutic strategy managed remotely, optimizing efficacy and safety while reducing hospital visits.

ideas.title: Dual-Pathogen: Minimize diagnostic device insertions for occult infection detection in chronic pain
ideas.description: This research program develops a clinical decision algorithm for chronic pain patients that treats the search for an occult infectious trigger as an optimization problem. The algorithm integrates patient history, imaging, and non-invasive biomarkers to prioritize anatomical sites for sampling, aiming for the fewest necessary invasive device insertions (e.g., biopsy needles) to achieve a target probability of pathogen detection. It formally balances the risk of procedural complications against the likelihood of identifying a treatable microbial cause, creating a personalized, risk-aware diagnostic pathway. This transforms the systematic search from a potentially exhaustive, high-burden process into a targeted, efficient, and ethically optimized clinical protocol.

ideas.title: Simulation-Optimized Diagnostic Cascade for Investigating Infectious Etiologies in Chronic Pain
ideas.description: This research develops a discrete-event simulation model to design and optimize diagnostic cascades for investigating infectious agents in idiopathic pain syndromes. The model incorporates variables such as test sensitivity/specificity, turnaround times, cost, and required specialist consultations to simulate patient flow and resource utilization. It allows for the evaluation of different diagnostic strategies (e.g., syndromic PCR panels vs. sequential serology) to identify the most efficient pathways for confirming or ruling out infectious etiologies within real-world operational limits. The outcome is a validated simulation tool that guides clinical research protocols by maximizing diagnostic yield while rationally managing laboratory, staffing, and financial resources.

ideas.title: Multi-Criteria Immunological Profiling for Stratified Management of Post-Infection Pain Syndromes
ideas.description: This research applies Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) to create a structured, quantitative framework for comparing and integrating heterogeneous immunological data from patients with fibromyalgia, IBS, and neuropathic pain triggered by infection. The core innovation is using MCDA to weigh the clinical severity, prognostic value, and economic implications (e.g., cost of biomarker testing, treatment accessibility) of specific immune pathways like bystander activation versus molecular mimicry. This enables the generation of a patient stratification tool that recommends personalized diagnostic and therapeutic strategies based on an optimized balance of immunological profile, predicted treatment response, and healthcare resource utilization, moving beyond descriptive mechanism comparison to actionable clinical decision support.

ideas.title: A Dynamic Risk-Prediction Model for Midline Catheter Dwell Time Guidance
ideas.description: This research aims to develop and validate a machine learning model that predicts the real-time, patient-specific risk of midline catheter failure or complication. Instead of a universal dwell time, the model integrates multimodal data, including serial vascular ultrasound, biomarkers of endothelial irritation and infection, infusion characteristics, and patient comorbidities. The output is a continuously updated risk score that clinicians can use to make individualized decisions for catheter removal or maintenance. This approach moves from reactive, time-based guidelines to proactive, physiology-based precision care.

ideas.title: Patient-Specific Risk Models for Device-Related Infection Incorporating Pre-Existing Pain as a Causal Immunomodulator
ideas.description: This research direction develops patient-specific predictive models for vascular access device complications by integrating the novel causal hypothesis that pre-existing chronic pain is a key immunomodulatory risk factor. It will test whether chronic pain, through measured alterations in immune function (e.g., cytokine profiles, leukocyte responsiveness), directly increases susceptibility to subsequent device-related infections. The resulting models will not only predict individual risk but will also identify the specific biological mechanism (pain-induced immune phenotype) driving that risk for a given patient. This enables targeted, pre-emptive interventions (e.g., pain management, immunomodulatory therapy, or heightened surveillance) tailored to an individual's mechanistic risk profile, rather than relying solely on demographic or clinical history.

ideas.title: Vascular Access Composite Harm Index (VACHI): A Severity-Weighted Metric for Device Evaluation
ideas.description: This research proposes the development and validation of a Vascular Access Composite Harm Index (VACHI) to replace singular, equivalent outcome metrics in device evaluations. VACHI integrates multiple complication types—including CLABSI, symptomatic thrombosis, dislodgement, and infiltrations—by assigning severity weights derived from data on patient pain, extravasation injury, treatment costs, and care delays. The system requires the prior establishment of a consensus, objective grading scale for infiltration severity to ensure consistent measurement. This approach provides a single, robust, and clinically meaningful score that reflects the true total burden of device-related harm, enabling more accurate comparisons between vascular access technologies and practices.

ideas.title: Structured, Asynchronous Multi-Disciplinary Device Selection Protocol for Complex Cases
ideas.description: This redesigned approach moves beyond a simple mandate for team discussion to a formalized, digitally-facilitated protocol. For pre-defined complex cases, the electronic health record triggers a mandatory device selection task that requires documented input from key disciplines based on patient-specific factors, such as wound type, mobility, and infection risk. Contributors provide input asynchronously against standardized criteria within a set timeframe, and the system synthesizes perspectives, highlighting consensus and disagreements for final review by the attending clinician. This ensures multidisciplinary expertise directly informs the decision in an auditable, efficient manner that integrates into clinical workflow rather than obstructing it.

ideas.title: A Diagnostic Test Framework for Validating Neural Circuitry as the Primary Driver in Post-Infection Chronic Pain
ideas.description: This research program applies the formal evaluation framework of a diagnostic test to the clinical challenge of distinguishing between pain driven by ongoing immune activity versus maladaptive neural circuitry. The 'decision algorithm' is a multi-modal clinical profile (e.g., specific symptom patterns, quantitative sensory testing, functional neuroimaging signatures) hypothesized to indicate a neural-circuit-predominant (NCP) state. This algorithm is tested against a gold standard of biomarkers for active infection or inflammation (e.g., pathogen-specific T-cells, cytokine panels, targeted imaging). A high specificity of the clinical algorithm would indicate it can correctly identify patients without ongoing immune activity, providing robust evidence that pain can be maintained by neural circuitry alone. This directly tests the core challenge of Idea B using the rigorous, binary classification methodology of Idea A.

ideas.title: Dynamic Risk-Weighted Decision Support for Vascular Access
ideas.description: This research synthesizes Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) with Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) to create a dynamic decision-support model for vascular access. The process begins with a traditional FMEA to identify potential failure modes in the placement process and device selection. Each failure mode is then evaluated not just by its Risk Priority Number, but by how its occurrence and severity would impact a comprehensive MCDA value function that incorporates clinical outcomes, patient safety, cost, and operational efficiency. This allows clinicians and administrators to prioritize mitigation efforts specifically on the failure modes that most severely degrade the overall multi-criteria performance of the vascular access pathway, leading to targeted, value-based quality improvement.

ideas.title: Dynamic, Real-Time Risk Monitoring and Alerting System for Vascular Access Complications
ideas.description: This research develops an integrated clinical decision support system that combines an initial patient-specific risk prediction with continuous, adaptive monitoring. The system processes real-time data streams from both the vascular access device itself and standard patient monitors to dynamically update complication risk scores. It is designed to detect early signs of deterioration or device failure that static models would miss, generating context-aware alerts for nursing and medical staff. This enables proactive, timely interventions tailored to the patient's evolving physiological state, moving beyond one-time prediction to active risk management throughout the device dwell time.

ideas.title: A Mandatory Clinical Engineering Review Board for High-Risk Medical Device Selection
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption that device selection is primarily a nursing decision by instituting a mandatory, structured, multi-disciplinary review process for complex cases, analogous to aerospace certification boards. Drawing from aerospace engineering, where critical component selection requires formal approval by a multi-disciplinary review board against strict criteria, we adapt this to create a 'Clinical Engineering Review Board' (CERB). For high-risk medical scenarios, device choice is removed from any single discipline's purview and made contingent on formal CERB approval, which must document consensus that the selected device meets protocol-defined standards for patient safety, clinical efficacy, cost-effectiveness, and operational feasibility before it can be ordered or used.

ideas.title: Contextualized Multi-Disciplinary Algorithmic Governance for Medical Device Selection
ideas.description: This research challenges the sufficiency of both isolated nursing decisions and rigid algorithm adherence by proposing a mandatory, structured multi-disciplinary team (MDT) process for complex device selection. The MDT's primary function is to explicitly contextualize a published selection algorithm to the local institution's resources, patient population, and team capabilities, creating a locally validated protocol. This transforms the algorithm from a static document into a dynamic, living guideline whose 'adherence' is defined by the team's consensus on necessary and documented adaptations. The outcome measures shift from simple device choice or algorithm checkbox compliance to the quality of the contextualization process and the resulting patient-centered, resource-aware outcomes.

ideas.title: Prospective validation of vascular access algorithms with psychophysiological stress as a primary outcome modifier
ideas.description: This research conducts prospective clinical trials to validate vascular access device selection algorithms, but with a critical novel focus: it prospectively investigates whether acute psychological stress during or after the procedure is a primary and sufficient factor to reactivate latent pain or discomfort from prior infections or interventions at the access site. The study will enroll diverse patient populations, stratifying them by psychological stress biomarkers and history, to determine if current algorithmic decisions (e.g., device type, insertion site) require modification for patients highly susceptible to stress-mediated pain reactivation. This fusion moves beyond validating technical algorithm accuracy to testing a fundamental hypothesis about a key biopsychosocial moderator of procedural success, potentially leading to the first stress-informed vascular access protocol.

ideas.title: Adapting Environmental Impact Assessment MCDA for Medical Device Selection
ideas.description: This research adapts the mature multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA) frameworks used in environmental impact assessments (EIAs) to the problem of medical device selection. In environmental science, MCDA successfully balances hard quantitative data (e.g., pollution levels, cost) with soft qualitative stakeholder values (e.g., community impact, long-term sustainability) for major projects. The adapted solution involves developing a formal medical device selection framework that integrates clinical evidence and economic factors with systematically captured qualitative criteria—such as patient-reported outcome expectations, clinical workflow integration, and institutional ethical priorities—using weighted decision models. The output is a standardized, transparent 'clinical device impact statement,' analogous to an environmental impact statement, which documents and justifies the selection process for auditability and stakeholder communication.

ideas.title: Comprehensive Probabilistic Algorithm Assessment via Clinical Utility Curves
ideas.description: This framework evaluates a decision algorithm not as a single binary test but as a probabilistic tool. It generates a suite of performance curves—including ROC, calibration, and decision curves—to visualize trade-offs between true and false positives across all possible thresholds. The system quantifies the algorithm's net benefit against default strategies, directly linking statistical performance to clinical or operational utility. This provides a robust, multi-faceted assessment essential for deployment in high-stakes environments.

ideas.title: Define duration of chronic suppressive therapy
ideas.description: Investigate the required length of posaconazole suppression to prevent recurrence after surgical intervention.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Case of Scopulariopsis brevicaulis Endocarditis with Mycotic Aneurysm in an Immunocompetent Host

ideas.title: Mandatory Multi-Disciplinary Device Selection to Decouple Pain from Inflammation in Neural Circuitry
ideas.description: This research program inverts the standard nursing-led device selection process by mandating a formal multi-disciplinary team (MDT) decision for implantable neuromodulation devices in patients with post-infection chronic pain. The MDT's primary mandate is to adjudicate, using a pre-specified protocol, whether a given patient's pain is likely maintained by maladaptive neural circuitry in the absence of ongoing immune activity, making them a candidate for device therapy. This creates a natural experiment where the MDT's selection criteria and outcomes rigorously test the core hypothesis that pain can persist without active infection or inflammation. Consequently, device selection becomes a direct, operationalized test of a fundamental pain mechanism, generating high-quality prospective data on a defined phenotype while improving therapeutic targeting.

ideas.title: Scale down CLABSI prevention for resource-limited settings
ideas.description: Develop a minimalist, cost-effective prevention bundle for low-resource hospitals.
ideas.sourceTitle: Prevention of Central-Line Associated Bloodstream Infections: 2021 Update.

ideas.title: National Registry for Regenerative Therapy Outcomes to Establish Efficacy Benchmarks and Standardized Protocols
ideas.description: This initiative establishes a national outcomes registry specifically for regenerative therapies aimed at reversing nerve and tissue damage from prior infections. By aggregating and benchmarking longitudinal data on functional recovery, pain reduction, and biomarker changes across diverse patient populations and clinical sites, the registry moves beyond simply tracking devices to actively defining efficacy for biological repair. The resulting benchmarks will allow for the creation of standardized, stage-specific treatment guidelines and eligibility criteria, accelerating the translation of regenerative approaches from research into widespread, optimized clinical practice. This systematic, large-scale evidence generation is critical for shifting the therapeutic paradigm from managing chronic pain to actively reversing its underlying pathological cause.

ideas.title: Contextual Device-to-Therapy Mapping: Inferring Treatment and Diagnosis from Device Selection Within Clinical Pathways
ideas.description: This system addresses the ambiguity of device selection by integrating the vascular device data with the patient's pre-procedural clinical profile and the documented procedural intent. It models the decision pathway that led to the device choice, using this enriched context to probabilistically infer the actual therapy delivered and the confirmed patient condition. The approach treats device use as a dependent variable within a clinical workflow, not an independent predictor, significantly improving inference accuracy. This creates a robust inverse model of procedural decision-making rather than a simple device lookup.

ideas.title: Assess fear's impact on clinical decision-making quality
ideas.description: Investigate how heightened fear quantitatively affects healthcare workers' clinical judgments and patient care decisions.

ideas.title: Empirical Typology of Non-Linear Risk-Dwell Time Functions for Procedural Complications
ideas.description: This research moves beyond simply relaxing the linearity assumption to actively discover and validate the specific functional forms linking dwell time and complication risk. It employs a computational pipeline to segment procedural data by complication type, fitting and comparing multiple non-linear models (e.g., exponential, threshold, saturated) to identify the best empirical descriptor for each. The outcome is a clinically actionable typology that categorizes complications by their risk-time profile, enabling tailored procedural time limits and monitoring protocols based on the quantified, complication-specific risk trajectory.

ideas.title: Adapting Satellite Constellation Health Monitoring for Home-Based Vascular Access Care
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from aerospace engineering, specifically the autonomous health monitoring systems used for distributed satellite constellations, to the challenge of scaling down vascular access care to the home. In satellite operations, each unit in a remote, distributed fleet uses onboard sensors and diagnostic algorithms to monitor its status, with data relayed to a central station that prioritizes alerts, predicts failures, and triggers remote protocols. Adapted to home-based IV therapy, this involves engineering a system of low-cost, wearable sensors on the vascular access site to continuously monitor key physiological and device parameters. These sensors connect to a patient-held device (smartphone or dedicated hub) that runs localized decision algorithms to detect early signs of complications like infection, infiltration, or occlusion. The system transmits encrypted, prioritized data to a central nursing coordination platform. This platform uses predictive analytics on aggregated, anonymized data from all home-based patients to identify risk patterns, triage alerts for nurse intervention, and deliver step-by-step, adaptive safety protocols to patients or caregivers for immediate contingency management, thereby enabling safe, scalable, and proactive remote care.

ideas.title: Interactive, Preference-Sensitive MCDA for Shared Clinical Decision-Making in Device Selection
ideas.description: This redesigned system embeds a formal Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) framework within an interactive software platform designed for clinical shared decision-making. It allows clinicians and, where appropriate, patients to dynamically adjust the importance weights of clinical, economic, and patient-centric criteria, visualizing in real-time how these preferences alter the ranking of potential medical devices. The platform includes robust sensitivity analysis dashboards to highlight the stability of recommendations and identify critical trade-offs. By making the valuation process transparent and adaptable, it moves beyond a one-size-fits-all recommendation to generate a context-specific, ethically defensible, and collaboratively derived selection.

ideas.title: Context-Aware, Non-Linear Risk Algorithms for Procedural Timing
ideas.description: This research challenges the sufficiency of published procedural algorithms that prescribe fixed dwell times or sequences without accounting for local resource constraints (e.g., staffing, equipment) and, crucially, without incorporating nuanced, non-linear models of complication risk over time. It posits that optimal, safe outcomes require algorithms that are both adaptable to a facility's real-time capabilities and informed by evidence that risk does not increase uniformly—or sometimes even increases—with longer procedural times for all complication types. The novel direction is to synthesize these into a methodology for generating context-aware decision support tools that dynamically balance non-linear risk curves against available resources to recommend patient-specific procedural timelines, rather than assuming algorithmic adherence alone guarantees the best result.

ideas.title: Scale to pediatric and neonatal populations

ideas.title: Auditing Team Decisions via Inverse Inference: Validating Multidisciplinary Device Selection Against Implied Therapy Pathways
ideas.description: This research proposes a system where, for complex vascular cases requiring mandatory MDT device selection, every chosen device is fed into an inverse inference model. The model predicts the most probable intended therapy and patient condition profile implied by that device choice. The model's output is then compared against the MDT's documented rationale and the actual patient presentation. Significant discrepancies flag decisions for retrospective review, revealing potential cognitive biases, knowledge gaps, or communication failures within the team. This creates a closed-loop learning system that uses data-driven inference to strengthen the human collaborative decision-making process it is designed to govern.

ideas.title: Establishing a Predictive Model for Midline Catheter Dwell Time Using Biomaterial Degradation Principles
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from biomaterials science, where the lifespan of implantable polymers is defined by modeling material degradation kinetics. The analogous problem of determining the safe functional period for biodegradable stents is solved by correlating measurable material property changes (molecular weight, strength) with failure risk. Adapted to midline catheters, the study would treat the catheter as a biomaterial in the vascular environment. It would establish a time-dependent 'functional integrity profile' by serially measuring key catheter properties (e.g., surface thrombogenicity, structural integrity, patency) in simulated use and correlating these with clinical outcome data (thrombosis, infection, mechanical complication rates). The goal is to move beyond empirical dwell time limits and develop an evidence-based, predictive model that defines the optimal dwell time as the point where the catheter's degrading functional profile intersects a predetermined risk-benefit threshold, potentially allowing for personalized, condition-specific guidelines.

ideas.title: Adapting Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Analysis from Signal Detection for Algorithmic Decision Threshold Optimization
ideas.description: The original idea treats a decision algorithm as a diagnostic test to evaluate its sensitivity and specificity. By drawing an analogy to Signal Detection Theory, we can expand this methodological transfer. In signal detection, systems are evaluated using Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) analysis, which plots the true positive rate against the false positive rate across all decision thresholds to visualize the trade-off between detection and false alarms. Adapting this solution, we propose to evaluate a medical device's decision algorithm by generating its ROC curve. This involves systematically testing the algorithm's performance at various classification thresholds against a gold standard, calculating sensitivity and specificity at each point. The resulting curve and its Area Under the Curve (AUC) provide a robust, threshold-independent assessment of the algorithm's overall discriminatory power and guide the selection of the most appropriate operational threshold for clinical deployment.

ideas.title: Infiltration-Induced Neuropathic Pain (IINP) as a Major Device Complication Metric
ideas.description: This research direction posits that serious intravenous infiltration events should be elevated to a major complication metric, akin to CLABSI, not only for acute tissue damage but for their potential to initiate chronic neuropathic pain syndromes. It investigates the hypothesis that the mechanical and chemical trauma of infiltration can induce lasting maladaptive plasticity in peripheral and central neural circuits, maintaining pain long after the local inflammatory response resolves. The fusion challenges the current paradigm by seeking to decouple the assessment of device-related harm from active infection, instead focusing on neurological sequalae. This would establish a new evidence base for device design and clinical protocols aimed at preventing infiltration to mitigate long-term patient disability from chronic pain.

ideas.title: Resilient Clinical System Design: Integrating Device Ergonomics with Workflow and Environmental Engineering
ideas.description: This research moves beyond device-centric human factors to adopt a systems engineering approach for medical device safety. It begins with in-depth cognitive task analysis and ethnographic observation in real clinical environments to map the entire work system, including workflow pressures, communication patterns, and environmental distractions. The design process then concurrently engineers the physical device for ergonomics, the procedural protocols for robustness, and digital support tools (e.g., seamless EHR integration, context-aware alerts) to create a unified, error-resistant system. The final output is a validated clinical system where the device, its supporting infrastructure, and the user's workflow are co-designed to maintain safety and usability under realistic conditions of stress and interruption.

ideas.title: Applying HAZOP from Chemical Process Safety to Vascular Access Placement
ideas.description: This research seed proposes adapting the Hazard and Operability (HAZOP) study methodology from chemical process safety engineering to proactively identify and mitigate risks in the vascular access placement process. In chemical engineering, HAZOP uses guided, systematic brainstorming with a multidisciplinary team and standardized 'guide words' to uncover potential deviations from a process's intended design. Adapted to vascular access, a clinical team would apply medically contextualized guide words to each procedural step to identify deviations (e.g., 'NO' anesthesia, 'MORE' anticoagulation, 'OTHER THAN' the intended vessel), their causes, consequences, and existing safeguards. This approach aims to generate a deeper, more operationally focused risk assessment than a traditional Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA), potentially revealing novel, complex failure pathways rooted in human factors and procedural variability.

ideas.title: Economic Inference Modeling: Using Device Choice to Predict Cost Trajectories and Optimize Resource Allocation
ideas.description: This research synthesizes cost-effectiveness analysis with inverse inference to create a predictive economic model. By analyzing real-world device selection patterns (e.g., midline, PICC, PIVC), the model first infers the likely underlying therapy and patient condition. It then uses this inferred clinical context to perform a dynamic, patient-stratified cost-effectiveness evaluation, comparing the economic outcomes of the chosen device against plausible alternatives. This approach reveals whether current device utilization patterns are economically rational given the inferred clinical needs and identifies high-cost scenarios where suboptimal device selection may be occurring, enabling targeted stewardship interventions.

ideas.title: Socio-Technical Simulation for Dynamic Risk Assessment in Vascular Access
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond a static FMEA by using repeated, high-fidelity in-situ simulations of vascular access placement to uncover real-world adaptive behaviors and system vulnerabilities. Clinical teams perform the procedure in their actual environment under realistic constraints, while observers document deviations, workarounds, and resource conflicts. The analysis from these simulations directly informs the co-design of embedded safety interventions, such as just-in-time checklist prompts or equipment setup guides, which are then stress-tested in subsequent simulation cycles before implementation.

ideas.title: Longer dwell time inherently increases risk

ideas.title: Stratum: Personalized risk-budgeting for device change pathways
ideas.description: This approach optimizes device change schedules by integrating a dynamic, patient-specific risk model rather than a population-level complication ceiling. For each patient, it estimates the probabilistic risk of adverse events for any potential insertion or removal procedure based on their unique clinical profile and current context. The system then computes a pathway that minimizes the total number of device changes while ensuring the sum of estimated risks across all procedures stays within a pre-defined, personalized risk budget. This creates a robust, safety-aware optimization that adapts to individual patient vulnerability and prevents high-risk interventions merely to reduce frequency.

The system continuously ingests real-time data on patient flow, device status, and staff availability to maintain a living model of operations. It employs online learning algorithms to dynamically adjust parameters, ensuring the model reflects current conditions. The core function is to stress-test inventory and staffing plans against a library of potential disruptions—from supply chain delays to disease outbreaks—providing decision-support for robust, resilient resource allocation.
